questions.q: What is payroll and why is it important for a business?
questions.a: Payroll is the process of compensating employees for their work and involves calculating wages, withholding taxes, deducting benefits, and issuing payments. It is critical because it ensures legal compliance with tax laws, maintains employee trust, and records labor costs accurately in financial statements. Errors in payroll can lead to IRS penalties, state fines, and lawsuits.
questions.highlight: Payroll = Gross Pay − Pre-tax deductions − Taxes − Post-tax deductions = Net Pay

questions.q: What is the difference between gross pay and net pay?
questions.a: Gross Pay is the total amount earned before any deductions — including base salary/hourly wages, overtime, bonuses, and commissions. Net Pay (take-home pay) is what the employee actually receives after all federal taxes, state taxes, FICA, health insurance premiums, 401(k) contributions, and other deductions are subtracted from gross pay.
questions.highlight: Net Pay = Gross Pay − All Deductions (Taxes + Benefits + Garnishments)

questions.q: What is a pay period and what types are commonly used in the USA?
questions.a:
A pay period is the recurring timeframe for which employees are paid. Common types:
  • Weekly – 52 pay periods/year (common in construction, manufacturing)
  • Bi-weekly – 26 pay periods/year (most common)
  • Semi-monthly – 24 pay periods/year (common in professional services)
  • Monthly – 12 pay periods/year (rare for hourly workers)
questions.highlight: Bi-weekly is the most common pay frequency in the USA.

questions.q: What is the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA)?
questions.a: The FLSA is the federal law that establishes minimum wage, overtime pay eligibility, recordkeeping requirements, and child labor standards. It classifies workers as exempt (salaried, no overtime) or non-exempt (eligible for overtime at 1.5x regular rate for hours over 40 in a workweek). As of 2024, the federal minimum wage is $7.25/hour, though many states have higher minimums.
questions.highlight: Non-exempt employees must receive 1.5x pay for hours worked over 40 in a single workweek.

questions.q: What is overtime pay and how is it calculated?
questions.a: Overtime is additional compensation for non-exempt employees who work more than 40 hours in a workweek. The federal rate is 1.5 times the regular rate of pay. Example: Employee earns $20/hr, works 45 hours. Regular pay = 40 × $20 = $800. Overtime = 5 × $30 = $150. Gross = $950. Some states (CA, AK) require daily overtime beyond 8 hours.
questions.highlight: Overtime Rate = Regular Rate × 1.5 (for hours > 40/week under FLSA)

questions.q: What is a W-4 form and what is its purpose?
questions.a: Form W-4 (Employee's Withholding Certificate) is completed by employees when they join a company. It tells the employer how much federal income tax to withhold from paychecks. The 2020 redesigned W-4 eliminated allowances and now uses filing status, multiple jobs adjustments, dependents, and other income/deductions to determine withholding. Employers must keep W-4s on file.
questions.highlight: W-4 determines federal income tax withholding — employees can update it any time.

questions.q: What is a W-2 form and when must it be issued?
questions.a: Form W-2 (Wage and Tax Statement) reports an employee's annual wages and taxes withheld. Employers must send W-2s to employees and file with the SSA by January 31 of the following year. W-2 includes Box 1 (wages), Box 2 (federal tax withheld), Box 3-6 (Social Security and Medicare wages/taxes), and state tax information. Employees use it to file their personal tax returns.
questions.highlight: W-2 deadline: January 31 to employees AND SSA. Penalty: $60–$630 per form.

questions.q: What is a 1099-NEC form?
questions.a: Form 1099-NEC (Nonemployee Compensation) is used to report payments of $600 or more made to independent contractors, freelancers, or self-employed individuals during the tax year. It replaced the old Box 7 of Form 1099-MISC starting in 2020. The issuer must file with the IRS and send to the recipient by January 31. No federal taxes are withheld on 1099 payments.
questions.highlight: 1099-NEC threshold: $600+ paid to any independent contractor in a calendar year.

questions.q: What is the Employer Identification Number (EIN)?
questions.a: An EIN (also called Federal Tax ID Number) is a unique 9-digit number (format: XX-XXXXXXX) assigned by the IRS to businesses for tax purposes. It is required for filing payroll taxes, opening business bank accounts, hiring employees, and filing business tax returns. Obtained free from the IRS via Form SS-4 or online at IRS.gov. State EINs/account numbers are separate.
questions.highlight: EIN format: 12-3456789. Required before hiring first employee and filing payroll taxes.

questions.q: What is a payroll register?
questions.a: A payroll register is a detailed record of all payroll transactions for a specific pay period. It lists every employee's gross pay, individual deductions (taxes, benefits, garnishments), and net pay. It serves as the master documentation for payroll, supports journal entries, and is used in audits. Modern payroll software generates this automatically. It must be retained for 3+ years per IRS requirements.
questions.highlight: Payroll register = complete snapshot of one pay period for all employees.

questions.q: What is the difference between a salary and hourly worker?
questions.a: Salaried employees receive a fixed annual amount divided across pay periods, typically exempt from overtime. Hourly workers are paid per hour worked, must track time, and are usually non-exempt (overtime-eligible). In payroll, salaried exempt employees have consistent paychecks while hourly employees vary based on hours worked. FLSA sets minimum salary thresholds ($684/week as of 2024) for overtime exemption.
questions.highlight: Salary threshold for FLSA exempt status: $684/week ($35,568/year) as of current rules.

questions.q: What is imputed income in payroll?
questions.a: Imputed income is the value of non-cash benefits provided to employees that must be included in their taxable gross income even though they don't receive it as cash. Examples include: group term life insurance over $50,000, personal use of company vehicle, employer-paid gym memberships, domestic partner health benefits. These amounts are added to W-2 Box 1 and are subject to income tax and FICA.
questions.highlight: Imputed income is taxable even though the employee receives a benefit, not cash.

questions.q: What is a fringe benefit and how is it treated for payroll tax purposes?
questions.a: Fringe benefits are extra compensation beyond regular wages — health insurance, retirement contributions, tuition reimbursement, meals, transportation benefits. Under IRS rules, most fringe benefits are included in taxable income unless specifically excluded by the tax code. Key exclusions: employer health insurance premiums (Section 106), 401(k) pre-tax contributions (Section 401), qualified transportation ($315/month in 2024).
questions.highlight: IRC Section 132 lists specific fringe benefits excluded from taxable income.

questions.q: What is a retro pay and how is it calculated?
questions.a: Retro (retroactive) pay is the difference between what an employee was paid and what they should have been paid — typically due to a pay raise that was applied late. Example: Employee should have received $25/hr from June 1 but was paid $22/hr. Retro pay = ($25 − $22) × hours worked in that period. It's taxable compensation and must be included in the paycheck of the next pay period with all applicable taxes withheld.
questions.highlight: Retro pay is taxable — all federal, state, and FICA taxes apply.

questions.q: What is a final paycheck and what are the laws around it?
questions.a: A final paycheck includes all unpaid wages through the last day worked, plus accrued PTO if state law requires payout. Federal law (FLSA) doesn't specify timing, but states do: California requires same-day payment for involuntary terminations; Texas requires next payroll date. Employers cannot withhold final checks to recover company property. Violations lead to penalties. Severance pay is separate from final wages.
questions.highlight: California is strictest: final paycheck due immediately upon termination.

questions.q: What is the difference between pay date and pay period end date?
questions.a: The pay period end date is the last day of the work period being compensated — this is when the employee stops accruing wages for that cycle. The pay date is the actual date the paycheck is issued/direct deposit hits. Typically there's a 3–7 day gap between pay period end and pay date to allow payroll processing time. These distinctions matter for accruals in accounting.
questions.highlight: Pay Period End ≠ Pay Date. The gap between them is the payroll processing window.

questions.q: What is a paid time off (PTO) accrual and how is it tracked?
questions.a:
PTO accrual is the gradual earning of paid leave over time. Common methods:
  • Per pay period: e.g., 3.08 hours per biweekly period = 80 hrs/year
  • Annual lump sum: Full balance given at start of year
  • Hourly accrual: Based on hours worked
Payroll systems track accrued, used, and available balances. Unused PTO may be a liability on the balance sheet if state law requires payout upon termination.
questions.highlight: PTO liability = Unused hours × Employee's current hourly rate.

questions.q: What is direct deposit and how does it work in payroll?
questions.a: Direct deposit is the electronic transfer of net pay directly into an employee's bank account via the ACH (Automated Clearing House) network. Employers submit payroll files to their bank or payroll processor 1–2 days before pay date. The bank routes funds to each employee's designated account. Employees provide a voided check or bank routing/account numbers on a direct deposit authorization form. It's the most common payment method in the USA.
questions.highlight: ACH transactions require 1-2 business days for processing — submit payroll early.

questions.q: What is supplemental wage withholding and what are the rates?
questions.a:
Supplemental wages are payments made in addition to regular wages — bonuses, commissions, overtime, back pay, sick pay, prizes. Two withholding methods:
  • Optional flat rate: 22% for supplemental wages under $1 million; 37% for amounts over $1 million in a year
  • Aggregate method: Add supplemental to regular wages, calculate tax on total, subtract regular withholding
Supplemental wages are also subject to FICA.
questions.highlight: Supplemental wage flat rate: 22% (under $1M) or 37% (over $1M annual threshold).

questions.q: What is the tip credit and how does it work under FLSA?
questions.a: The tip credit allows employers to pay tipped employees less than minimum wage if their tips bring total hourly pay to at least $7.25/hr. Federal tip credit = $5.12/hr, so the cash wage is $2.13/hr. If tips don't cover the gap, employer must make up the difference. Tips must be retained by the employee (tip pooling rules apply). 8 states prohibit tip credit (CA, OR, WA, MN, AK, NV, MT, HI). Employer must notify employees of tip credit usage.
questions.highlight: Federal tipped minimum wage = $2.13/hr. Employer guarantee: total must reach $7.25/hr.

questions.q: What is FMLA and does it affect payroll?
questions.a: The Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) allows eligible employees to take up to 12 weeks of unpaid, job-protected leave per year for qualifying reasons (birth of child, serious health condition, military family leave). Payroll impact: employer-paid benefits continue during FMLA, employee retains job. Payroll must track FMLA usage. Many employers allow concurrent use of PTO with FMLA. FMLA applies to employers with 50+ employees.
questions.highlight: FMLA = 12 weeks unpaid protected leave. Employer must maintain group health benefits.

questions.q: What is garnishment in payroll?
questions.a: Wage garnishment is a court-ordered deduction from an employee's paycheck to satisfy a debt — child support, student loans, back taxes, credit card debts. Federal law (Consumer Credit Protection Act) limits garnishment: generally 25% of disposable earnings OR the amount by which disposable earnings exceed 30× federal minimum wage, whichever is less. Child support has higher limits (50–65%). Employers must comply or face liability.
questions.highlight: Garnishment limit: 25% of disposable earnings, with child support allowed up to 65%.

questions.q: What is a pay stub and what information must it contain?
questions.a: A pay stub (earnings statement) details the calculation of an employee's paycheck. Required/recommended information: pay period dates, pay date, gross earnings breakdown, all deductions itemized (federal tax, state tax, Social Security, Medicare, benefits, garnishments), YTD totals, and net pay. Federal law doesn't require pay stubs, but many states do (CA, NY, TX, etc.). Electronic pay stubs are acceptable if employees can access them.
questions.highlight: YTD (Year-to-Date) figures on pay stubs help employees track annual totals for tax filing.

questions.q: What is workers' compensation insurance?
questions.a: Workers' comp is mandatory state insurance that provides wage replacement and medical benefits to employees injured on the job. Employers pay premiums based on payroll and job risk classification codes. Payroll is the premium base: Annual Premium ≈ (Payroll / 100) × Rate. Workers' comp is not a payroll tax but a payroll-related expense. Most states require it for all employers with 1+ employees. It's administered by state agencies or private insurers.
questions.highlight: Workers' comp premium = (Total Payroll ÷ 100) × Rate per $100 of payroll.

questions.q: What is a payroll audit and what does it examine?
questions.a: A payroll audit is a systematic review of payroll records to ensure accuracy, compliance, and prevent fraud. External audits (IRS, state agencies) examine: worker classification (employee vs contractor), proper tax withholding and remittance, correct wage rates, overtime calculations, I-9 documentation, W-2/1099 accuracy. Internal audits check: segregation of duties, ghost employees, unauthorized pay changes, benefit deductions. Triggered by complaints, random selection, or inconsistencies.
questions.highlight: Most common payroll audit triggers: worker misclassification, late deposits, unreported wages.

questions.q: What is the IRS Publication 15 (Circular E)?
questions.a: IRS Publication 15 (Employer's Tax Guide) is the essential reference manual for employers. It provides guidance on: federal income tax withholding methods, FICA rates and wage bases, deposit schedules, Forms 941/944 filing, taxable/non-taxable fringe benefits, and employment tax rules. Updated annually. KPO payroll professionals must be thoroughly familiar with Pub 15, which is available free on IRS.gov.
questions.highlight: Pub 15 = The employer's bible for federal payroll tax rules. Updated every January.

questions.q: What is meant by 'payroll reconciliation'?
questions.a:
Payroll reconciliation is the process of verifying that payroll records match tax filings, bank statements, and general ledger entries. Types:
  • Monthly: Compare payroll register to GL
  • Quarterly: Reconcile Form 941 to payroll records
  • Annual: W-2 amounts must match cumulative payroll and 941 filings
Discrepancies must be investigated and corrected. W-3 total must equal sum of all W-2s.
questions.highlight: Annual reconciliation: Sum of all W-2 Box 1 wages must equal total 941 Box 2 wages filed.

questions.q: What is a cafeteria plan (Section 125)?
questions.a: A Section 125 cafeteria plan allows employees to pay for certain benefits (health insurance, FSA, dependent care FSA) with pre-tax dollars, reducing both income taxes and FICA. Named for the 'menu' of benefits employees can choose from. Employer benefits: lower FICA matching. Employee benefits: reduced taxable income. Must be documented in a written plan. Premium-only plans (POPs) are the simplest type.
questions.highlight: Section 125 reduces both employee and employer FICA taxes — major savings vehicle.

questions.q: What is the look-back period for determining deposit schedule?
questions.a: The look-back period is the 12-month period from July 1 of the second preceding year through June 30 of the prior year. Employers who reported $50,000 or less in FICA and federal income tax during the look-back period are monthly depositors. Those who reported more than $50,000 are semi-weekly depositors. New employers default to monthly depositor status. Determined annually.
questions.highlight: Look-back period: July 1 (Year -2) to June 30 (Year -1) determines current year schedule.

questions.q: What is the $100,000 next-day deposit rule?
questions.a: If an employer accumulates $100,000 or more in payroll tax liability on any day during a deposit period (regardless of whether they're a monthly or semi-weekly depositor), they must deposit those taxes by the next banking day. Once this rule applies, the employer automatically becomes a semi-weekly depositor for the remainder of the year and the following year. This rule overrides normal deposit schedules.
questions.highlight: $100K accumulated in one day = next banking day deposit required, no exceptions.

questions.q: What does YTD stand for in payroll and why is it important?
questions.a: YTD stands for Year-to-Date — the cumulative amount from January 1 through the current pay date. Pay stubs show YTD gross earnings, YTD federal tax withheld, YTD FICA (SS and Medicare separately), and YTD state taxes. YTD is critical for: tracking when Social Security wage base ($168,600 in 2024) is reached, verifying annual withholding accuracy, preparing W-2s, and reconciling with 941 deposits.
questions.highlight: YTD Social Security wages capped at $168,600 for 2024. Once exceeded, SS withholding stops.

questions.q: What is the Affordable Care Act (ACA) impact on payroll?
questions.a:
ACA affects payroll in multiple ways:
  • Employer Mandate: ALEs (50+ FTE) must offer minimum essential coverage or pay penalties
  • Additional Medicare Tax: 0.9% on wages over $200K (single)/$250K (married)
  • W-2 Reporting: Employers with 250+ W-2s must report employer-sponsored health coverage in Box 12 Code DD
  • Forms 1094-C/1095-C: ALEs file annually to report coverage offered
questions.highlight: ALE = Applicable Large Employer (50+ full-time equivalent employees).

questions.q: What is a flexible spending account (FSA) and how is it handled in payroll?
questions.a: An FSA is a pre-tax account allowing employees to set aside money for eligible medical expenses or dependent care. Healthcare FSA limit: $3,200 (2024). Dependent Care FSA: $5,000 ($2,500 married filing separately). Contributions reduce federal income tax AND FICA. The 'use-it-or-lose-it' rule applies (with optional $640 rollover). In payroll, FSA deductions are pre-tax — reduces Box 1 but employer must still track separately.
questions.highlight: FSA 2024 limits: Healthcare = $3,200; Dependent Care = $5,000 per household.

questions.q: What is an HSA (Health Savings Account) in payroll context?
questions.a: An HSA is a tax-advantaged savings account for medical expenses, available only to employees enrolled in a High-Deductible Health Plan (HDHP). HSA contributions are triple-tax-advantaged: pre-tax contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for medical expenses. 2024 limits: $4,150 (self-only), $8,300 (family), +$1,000 catch-up for 55+. Employer HSA contributions are excluded from income and FICA. Employee payroll contributions are pre-tax via Section 125.
questions.highlight: HSA triple tax advantage: pre-tax contribution + tax-free growth + tax-free withdrawal.

questions.q: What is a 401(k) and how is it processed in payroll?
questions.a: A 401(k) is an employer-sponsored retirement savings plan under IRC Section 401(k). Employee contributions are pre-tax (Traditional) or post-tax (Roth). Payroll deduction: Traditional 401(k) reduces federal taxable income (Box 1) but NOT FICA wages. 2024 limits: $23,000 employee contribution; $69,000 total (employee + employer). Employer match is a payroll expense recorded separately. Contributions are remitted to the plan administrator by specific deadlines.
questions.highlight: 401(k) pre-tax contributions reduce income tax, NOT FICA. Roth 401(k) reduces neither.

questions.q: What is a safe harbor 401(k)?
questions.a:
A safe harbor 401(k) is a plan design that automatically satisfies IRS nondiscrimination testing (ADP/ACP tests) by providing mandatory employer contributions. Options:
  • Basic match: 100% on first 3% + 50% on next 2% of salary
  • Enhanced match: 100% on first 4%
  • Non-elective: 3% of compensation for all eligible employees
In payroll, safe harbor contributions are 100% vested immediately. Notification to employees required 30 days before plan year.
questions.highlight: Safe harbor = Automatic nondiscrimination compliance in exchange for mandatory employer contributions.

questions.q: What is COBRA and its payroll implications?
questions.a: COBRA (Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act) requires employers with 20+ employees to offer continuation of group health coverage after qualifying events (termination, hours reduction, divorce, etc.) for 18–36 months. The employee pays the full premium (up to 102% of cost). Payroll impact: upon termination, stop benefit deductions; send COBRA election notice within 44 days; employer may still collect premiums and remit to insurer. KPO teams often manage COBRA billing.
questions.highlight: COBRA election notice: must be sent within 44 days of qualifying event.

questions.q: What is the difference between pre-tax and post-tax deductions?
questions.a: Pre-tax deductions are taken from gross pay before taxes are calculated, reducing taxable income. Examples: 401(k) Traditional, health/dental/vision premiums (Section 125), FSA/HSA, commuter benefits. Post-tax deductions are taken after all taxes are calculated on the full gross pay. Examples: Roth 401(k), supplemental life insurance, wage garnishments, voluntary charity. The order matters for calculating the correct withholding.
questions.highlight: Deduction order: Gross → Pre-tax deductions → Taxable wages → Taxes → Post-tax deductions → Net Pay

questions.q: What are employer payroll taxes?
questions.a:
Employer payroll taxes are costs paid by the employer (not withheld from employees) as a percentage of payroll. These include:
  • Employer FICA: 6.2% Social Security + 1.45% Medicare = 7.65%
  • FUTA: 6% on first $7,000 (usually 0.6% after state credit)
  • SUTA: Varies by state (typically 0.5%–5.4%)
  • Workers' Comp: Based on payroll and risk class
Total employer cost is roughly 18–25% above gross wages.
questions.highlight: Employer burden: +7.65% FICA + FUTA/SUTA + workers' comp + benefits on top of gross wages.

questions.q: What is the purpose of Form I-9 in payroll/HR?
questions.a: Form I-9 (Employment Eligibility Verification) is required for every new hire in the USA. It verifies the employee's identity and authorization to work in the United States. Completed by employee (Section 1) on or before Day 1, and employer (Section 2) within 3 business days of start date. Must be retained 3 years from hire date OR 1 year after termination, whichever is later. E-Verify is an optional electronic verification system. ICE can audit I-9s.
questions.highlight: I-9 retention: 3 years from hire OR 1 year after termination — keep the later date.

questions.q: What is a pay card (payroll card) and how is it treated?
questions.a: A payroll card is a prepaid debit card onto which net pay is directly deposited — an alternative to direct deposit or paper checks. Useful for unbanked employees. Regulated by the Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA) and state laws. From a payroll perspective, treated identically to direct deposit. Employers cannot require payroll cards; employees must have the option of alternative payment. Some states (NY, CA) have specific disclosure requirements.
questions.highlight: Payroll cards = prepaid debit cards. EFTA governs; employers cannot mandate their use.

questions.q: What is the employer's responsibility for payroll tax deposits when using a PEO?
questions.a: A Professional Employer Organization (PEO) co-employs workers, typically becoming the employer of record for payroll tax purposes. Under co-employment: the PEO withholds and remits payroll taxes under its own EIN (not the client's). The client company is still ultimately responsible if the PEO fails to remit taxes. IRS Notice 2013-54 clarifies PEO responsibilities. Certified PEOs (CPEOs) provide greater liability protection for clients.
questions.highlight: Even with a PEO, client company retains ultimate liability if payroll taxes aren't remitted.

questions.q: What is the significance of pay period end date for accruals?
questions.a: In accrual accounting, wages earned by employees but not yet paid at period end must be recorded as an accrual. Example: Pay period ends Dec 28, payday is Jan 3. Wages for Dec 29–31 are an expense in December but paid in January. Entry: Debit Wages Expense, Credit Wages Payable. This ensures expenses are matched to the period they were earned (matching principle). Accrued payroll is a current liability on the balance sheet.
questions.highlight: Wages earned but unpaid at month-end = Debit Wage Expense / Credit Wages Payable.

questions.q: What is the difference between exempt and non-exempt employees?
questions.a: Exempt employees are not entitled to overtime under FLSA. Must meet salary basis test ($684/week minimum) AND duties test (executive, administrative, professional, computer, outside sales exemptions). Non-exempt employees are entitled to overtime pay (1.5x) for hours over 40/week. Must track all hours. Most hourly workers are non-exempt. Misclassification is one of the most common and costly payroll errors.
questions.highlight: FLSA exemption requires BOTH the salary test ($684/week) AND the duties test.

questions.q: What is unlimited PTO and how does it affect payroll accounting?
questions.a: Unlimited PTO (UPTO) policies allow employees to take as much paid time off as they need without accruing a specific balance. From an accounting perspective, UPTO eliminates PTO liability on the balance sheet because there's no defined accrued balance to pay out upon termination. However, UPTO doesn't mean no liability in all states — California courts have sometimes treated UPTO differently. HR and payroll must coordinate policy documentation carefully.
questions.highlight: Unlimited PTO = No accrued balance = No PTO liability on balance sheet (generally).

questions.q: What is a shift differential in payroll?
questions.a: A shift differential is additional pay for working less desirable shifts — evenings, nights, weekends, or holidays. It can be expressed as a flat rate per hour (e.g., $2/hr extra) or a percentage of base pay (e.g., 10% more). In payroll calculation, shift differentials are included in gross pay and are taxable. For overtime calculations, the blended rate including differential must be used to correctly compute the regular rate of pay for FLSA purposes.
questions.highlight: Shift differential hours affect the 'regular rate of pay' calculation for overtime.

questions.q: What is a common-law employee vs. statutory employee?
questions.a: A common-law employee is someone whose work details are controlled by the employer (what to do and how to do it). Standard employment tax rules apply. A statutory employee is an independent contractor by common law but treated as an employee for FICA purposes under IRC. Four categories: full-time life insurance salespeople, certain agent/commission drivers, home workers, and traveling salespeople. They get a W-2 with Box 13 'Statutory employee' checked. They deduct business expenses on Schedule C.
questions.highlight: Statutory employees get W-2 but can deduct business expenses like self-employed individuals.

questions.q: What is a PEO (Professional Employer Organization)?
questions.a: A PEO is a firm that provides HR outsourcing — payroll, benefits, workers' comp, and HR compliance — through a co-employment arrangement. The PEO becomes the employer of record for tax purposes, allowing small businesses to access Fortune 500-level benefits. PEOs file W-2s under their EIN. The client company retains control over day-to-day operations. NAPEO estimates PEOs save businesses 27.2% on HR admin costs. Major PEOs: ADP TotalSource, Insperity, TriNet.
questions.highlight: PEO = Co-employer handling payroll/taxes/benefits under their EIN.

questions.q: What is a blended overtime rate and when is it used?
questions.a: A blended (weighted average) overtime rate is required when an employee works two or more jobs at different rates during the same workweek and exceeds 40 hours. Method 1 (blended rate): Total compensation for week ÷ Total hours = Regular rate. Overtime premium = 0.5 × Regular rate × Overtime hours. Method 2 (rate in effect): Pay overtime at the rate of the job being performed during overtime hours (requires employee agreement). The default FLSA method is the blended rate.
questions.highlight: Blended rate = Total straight-time pay ÷ Total hours. Then add 0.5× for OT hours only.

questions.q: What records must employers retain for payroll and how long?
questions.a:
Federal retention requirements:
  • Payroll records (FLSA): 3 years from last effective date
  • Time cards, work schedules: 2 years
  • W-4 forms: 4 years after tax due
  • W-2 file copies: 4 years
  • I-9 forms: 3 years from hire or 1 year post-termination
  • Tax deposits, 941s: 4 years
Many states have longer requirements. EEOC recommends 1 year. Best practice: 7 years for everything payroll-related.
questions.highlight: Safe practice: Retain all payroll records for 7 years to satisfy both federal and state laws.

questions.q: What are the two main methods of calculating federal income tax withholding?
questions.a: 1. Wage Bracket Method: Uses IRS Publication 15-T tables. Match employee's adjusted wages and pay period to find the tentative withholding amount. Simple and quick. 2. Percentage Method: More precise, works for any wage amount. Adjust wages per W-4 instructions, then apply the tax rate schedule from Pub 15-T. Modern payroll software always uses the percentage method. Both methods yield similar results when properly applied.
questions.highlight: Payroll software uses the Percentage Method from Pub 15-T for accurate calculations.

questions.q: What are the 2024 federal income tax brackets for a single filer?
questions.a:
2024 federal income tax brackets (single):
  • 10%: $0–$11,600
  • 12%: $11,601–$47,150
  • 22%: $47,151–$100,525
  • 24%: $100,526–$191,950
  • 32%: $191,951–$243,725
  • 35%: $243,726–$609,350
  • 37%: Over $609,350
The US uses a progressive system — only income within each bracket is taxed at that rate. Withholding approximates these brackets based on W-4 information.
questions.highlight: 2024 standard deduction: $14,600 (single), $29,200 (married filing jointly).

questions.q: What is the Percentage Method Tables for Automated Payroll Systems?
questions.a: Pub 15-T provides two Percentage Method tables: one for Forms W-4 from 2020+ and one for pre-2020 W-4s. Process for 2020+ W-4: (1) Adjust employee's wage by adding additional withholding amounts from Step 3 and 4. (2) Find the tentative withholding from the appropriate table based on pay period and filing status. (3) Subtract credits from Step 3. This is the federal income tax to withhold. KPO teams must be familiar with both tables.
questions.highlight: Always verify which W-4 version (pre-2020 or post-2020) an employee has on file.

questions.q: What are the W-4 Step instructions for 2020+ form?
questions.a:
The redesigned W-4 has 5 steps:
  • Step 1: Personal info + filing status (required)
  • Step 2: Multiple jobs adjustment (optional but important for accuracy)
  • Step 3: Claim dependents — children under 17 ($2,000 each), other dependents ($500 each)
  • Step 4a: Other income (not from jobs)
  • Step 4b: Deductions above standard
  • Step 4c: Additional withholding per period
Only Step 1 and signature required. Others optional.
questions.highlight: Steps 2–4 on W-4 are optional but improve withholding accuracy significantly.

questions.q: What happens if an employee doesn't submit a W-4?
questions.a: If an employee doesn't submit a W-4 (or submits an incomplete one), the employer must withhold federal income tax as if the employee is single with no adjustments — the highest withholding rate for a given wage. This was called 'zero allowances' under the old system. The employer should request a W-4 but can continue withholding at the default rate. Employees can update their W-4 at any time during the year.
questions.highlight: No W-4 on file = Withhold as Single with no adjustments (highest rate for that wage).

questions.q: What is a lock-in letter from the IRS?
questions.a: An IRS lock-in letter (CP 2100/2100A) instructs an employer to withhold at a specific rate for a named employee who has been under-withholding. The employer must implement the lock-in rate by the date specified (usually within 60 days). The employee cannot change their W-4 to reduce withholding below the lock-in rate unless the IRS approves. The employer must notify the employee within 10 days of receiving the letter. Ignoring lock-in letters creates employer liability.
questions.highlight: Lock-in letter overrides employee's W-4. Must implement within 60 days of receipt.

questions.q: What is backup withholding?
questions.a: Backup withholding is 24% federal tax withheld from certain payments (interest, dividends, 1099 payments) when: the payee fails to furnish a TIN, provides an incorrect TIN, or the IRS notifies the payer to begin withholding. For payroll contractors: if a 1099 vendor doesn't provide a correct SSN/EIN on Form W-9, backup withholding at 24% applies to payments. Reported on Form 945 (Annual Return of Withheld Federal Income Tax).
questions.highlight: Backup withholding rate: 24%. Applies when payee lacks correct TIN. Reported on Form 945.

questions.q: What is the withholding exemption and when can an employee claim it?
questions.a: An employee can claim withholding exemption (write 'Exempt' in Step 4c on W-4) only if: (1) they had zero federal income tax liability in the prior year AND (2) they expect zero federal income tax liability in the current year. The exemption expires each February 15 — if not renewed, employer defaults to single with no adjustments. Cannot claim exempt if someone else can claim them as a dependent and they have income over $1,100 + $350.
questions.highlight: Exempt W-4 expires February 15 each year. Must be renewed annually if still applicable.

questions.q: How does the IRS Withholding Estimator help employees?
questions.a: The IRS Withholding Estimator (IRS.gov/W4App) is a free online tool allowing employees to check if they're withholding the right amount of federal income tax. It accounts for multiple jobs, spouse's income, side income, deductions, and tax credits. KPO professionals often advise clients to direct employees here during annual W-4 review campaigns, especially after life changes (marriage, new child, second job). Output: specific recommended W-4 entries.
questions.highlight: IRS Withholding Estimator = free tool to optimize W-4 accuracy for employees.

questions.q: What is the standard deduction and how does it affect withholding?
questions.a: The standard deduction is a flat dollar amount that reduces taxable income for taxpayers who don't itemize. 2024 amounts: $14,600 (single/MFS), $29,200 (MFJ/QSS), $21,900 (HOH). In withholding calculations, the annual wage is reduced by the standard deduction equivalent before applying tax brackets. Employees with itemized deductions above the standard deduction enter the difference in Step 4b of W-4 to adjust withholding.
questions.highlight: 2024 Standard Deduction: $14,600 (Single), $29,200 (MFJ), $21,900 (Head of Household).

questions.q: What are pre-tax deductions that reduce federal income tax withholding?
questions.a:
Pre-tax deductions reduce the federal taxable wage (W-2 Box 1) and include:
  • 401(k) / 403(b) Traditional contributions
  • Health/dental/vision premiums (under Section 125 cafeteria plan)
  • FSA contributions (healthcare and dependent care)
  • HSA contributions (via payroll)
  • Commuter benefits (transit/parking up to $315/month in 2024)
Note: These deductions do NOT reduce FICA wages (except for cafeteria plan deductions).
questions.highlight: Pre-tax deductions reduce income tax AND FICA (when through Section 125 plan).

questions.q: What is the Additional Medicare Tax and how is it withheld?
questions.a: The Additional Medicare Tax (AMT) is 0.9% on wages exceeding $200,000 for single filers ($250,000 for MFJ, $125,000 for MFS). Employers must begin withholding at the $200,000 threshold regardless of filing status — the IRS reconciles the threshold on the employee's individual return. Employers do NOT match this 0.9% (unlike regular Medicare). Reported in Box 6 of W-2. Employees owing more due to MFJ threshold pay on Form 1040.
questions.highlight: Withhold additional 0.9% Medicare when employee wages exceed $200K — employer threshold only.

questions.q: What is a safe harbor for under-withholding penalties?
questions.a: Individual taxpayers avoid underpayment penalties if they: (1) owe less than $1,000 after credits and withholding, (2) withheld at least 90% of current year tax, or (3) withheld 100% of prior year tax liability (110% if AGI over $150,000). For employers, the FTP (Failure to Pay) penalty safe harbor: deposits are timely if within 2% shortfall on payroll deposits. IRS allows a shortfall of up to $100 per deposit period without penalty.
questions.highlight: Individual safe harbor: 100% of prior-year tax (110% if AGI > $150K).

questions.q: What is Form W-2c and when is it used?
questions.a: Form W-2c (Corrected Wage and Tax Statement) is used to correct errors on previously filed W-2s. Also requires filing Form W-3c (transmittal). Common reasons: incorrect SSN, wrong name, wrong Box 1 wages, incorrect FICA amounts. If W-2 corrections affect FICA, may also need Form 941-X. W-2c must be provided to employee and filed with SSA. No strict deadline but should be corrected as soon as discovered. Multiple errors = multiple boxes corrected on single W-2c.
questions.highlight: W-2c corrects previously issued W-2s. File with SSA + send corrected copy to employee.

questions.q: What is the federal income tax withholding filing status?
questions.a:
Filing status determines the tax rate and standard deduction applied in withholding. W-4 options:
  • Single or MFS: Highest withholding rate
  • Married filing jointly (MFJ): Lower withholding rate
  • Head of Household (HOH): Between single and MFJ rates
Employees must self-certify their status. Employer cannot override the declared status unless IRS issues a lock-in letter. Filing status on W-4 doesn't have to match 1040 filing status.
questions.highlight: HOH status was added to the W-4 in 2020. Check it provides appropriate withholding.

questions.q: What is the employer's deposit schedule for federal income taxes?
questions.a:
Two main deposit schedules:
  • Monthly depositors: Deposit by 15th of following month. Used by employers with ≤$50,000 in prior look-back period
  • Semi-weekly depositors: Payday Wednesday-Friday → deposit by following Wednesday. Payday Saturday-Tuesday → deposit by following Friday
  • $100K rule: If $100K+ accumulated, next banking day regardless of schedule
New employers: monthly by default for first year.
questions.highlight: Semi-weekly: Wed-Fri paydays → next Wed deposit. Sat-Tue paydays → next Fri deposit.

questions.q: What is EFTPS?
questions.a: EFTPS (Electronic Federal Tax Payment System) is the IRS's free online system for making federal tax deposits electronically. All federal payroll tax deposits must be made through EFTPS (or via a payroll software integration). Enrollment requires 5–7 days for PIN delivery. Payments can be scheduled up to 365 days in advance. Available 24/7/365. Backup: IRS2Go app. Same-day wire transfer available through bank for last-minute deposits. No checks accepted for payroll deposits.
questions.highlight: EFTPS is mandatory for federal payroll tax deposits. Enroll at eftps.gov. Allow 5-7 days.

questions.q: What are the penalties for late payroll tax deposits?
questions.a:
IRS deposit penalties (FTD — Failure to Timely Deposit):
  • 1–5 days late: 2% penalty
  • 6–15 days late: 5% penalty
  • 16+ days late: 10% penalty
  • IRS notice issued and 10+ days after notice: 15% penalty
Penalty base = amount not timely deposited. Additionally, the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP) can hold individuals personally liable for 100% of employee-withheld taxes if willful failure to remit.
questions.highlight: Trust Fund Recovery Penalty = 100% of unremitted employee taxes assessed personally.

questions.q: What is the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP)?
questions.a: The TFRP (IRC Section 6672) holds 'responsible persons' personally liable for the 100% penalty on unremitted trust fund taxes (employee income tax and employee FICA withholdings). 'Responsible persons' include owners, CFOs, payroll managers, bookkeepers, or any person who had authority over payroll decisions. IRS must prove willfulness (knew of obligation, had ability to pay, chose not to). TFRP survives bankruptcy and can garnish personal wages/assets. Assessment statute: 3 years.
questions.highlight: TFRP can pierce the corporate veil — personally held liable for 100% of trust fund taxes.

questions.q: What is withholding on nonresident aliens (NRA)?
questions.a: Nonresident aliens (NRA) working in the USA are subject to special withholding rules. NRAs cannot claim exempt status, and employers must withhold at single with one additional amount (from Pub 15-T NRA Table). Many NRAs have tax treaties that reduce withholding — requires Form 8233 or W-8BEN. For wages, Form W-4 with NRA modification. For non-wage payments, 30% withholding applies (or treaty rate). W-2 or 1042-S issued depending on payment type.
questions.highlight: NRA employees: Use special W-4 withholding tables. Cannot claim exempt status.

questions.q: What is Form 1042 and Form 1042-S?
questions.a: Form 1042 (Annual Withholding Tax Return for US Source Income of Foreign Persons) is filed annually by withholding agents to report and pay taxes withheld on income paid to foreign persons. Form 1042-S (Foreign Person's US Source Income Subject to Withholding) is the information return given to foreign payees and filed with the IRS. Tax year 2024 deadline: March 15. Common in KPO firms managing US clients with foreign contractors or investors.
questions.highlight: Form 1042 + 1042-S: Annual filing by March 15 for withholding on payments to foreigners.

questions.q: What is a withholding agent?
questions.a: A withholding agent is any person (US or foreign) who has control, receipt, custody, disposal, or payment of an amount subject to withholding. In payroll, this is the employer. Withholding agents are legally responsible for: withholding the correct amount, depositing withheld taxes, filing required returns, and furnishing information statements. If the withholding agent fails to withhold, they can be held liable for the tax that should have been withheld.
questions.highlight: Withholding agent = employer. Legally responsible for correct withholding and remittance.

questions.q: How is withholding calculated for an employee with multiple jobs on the 2020+ W-4?
questions.a: Step 2 of the W-4 addresses multiple jobs. Options: (a) Use IRS online estimator for most accurate result; (b) Check the box in 2c (only if 2 jobs with similar pay — this roughly doubles withholding); (c) Use Multiple Jobs Worksheet (Worksheet 1 in W-4 instructions) to compute additional withholding to enter in Step 4c. The issue: each job withholds based on its wages alone, missing that combined income puts employee in higher bracket.
questions.highlight: Step 2 check-box = only good when both jobs pay similar wages. Use estimator for precision.

questions.q: What are nontaxable wages?
questions.a:
Certain payments are excluded from federal income tax withholding (and often FICA). Common nontaxable items:
  • Employer-paid health insurance premiums
  • Employer 401(k) contributions (matches)
  • Meals and lodging for employer convenience (IRC §119)
  • Educational assistance up to $5,250 (IRC §127)
  • De minimis fringe benefits (small gifts, occasional meals)
  • Qualified moving expense reimbursements (military only post-TCJA)
questions.highlight: IRC §132 and §119 are the key code sections for nontaxable fringe benefits.

questions.q: What is the difference between taxable and non-taxable reimbursements?
questions.a: Under an accountable plan, employee expense reimbursements are non-taxable if: (1) business connection exists, (2) employee substantiates with receipts within a reasonable period, (3) excess reimbursements are returned. Under a non-accountable plan, all reimbursements are taxable wages included in W-2 Box 1 and subject to FICA. Per diem and mileage rates above IRS limits are also taxable. Most common issue: auto allowances that aren't substantiated.
questions.highlight: Accountable plan reimbursements = non-taxable. Non-accountable = included in W-2 wages.

questions.q: What is the IRS standard mileage rate and how is it used in payroll?
questions.a: The IRS standard mileage rate (67 cents/mile in 2024) is used to calculate the deductible cost of using a personal vehicle for business. Employer reimbursements at or below the standard rate are non-taxable (with substantiation). Reimbursements above the standard rate are taxable for the excess. Many companies use actual expense reimbursement instead. For personal use of company vehicles, the cents-per-mile method or Annual Lease Value method applies for imputed income calculation.
questions.highlight: 2024 IRS Standard Mileage Rate: 67 cents/mile. Reimburse at/below this = non-taxable.

questions.q: What is the Golden Rule in tax withholding?
questions.a: The 'Golden Rule' of payroll withholding: the amount withheld should closely match the employee's actual tax liability. Under-withholding causes the employee to owe at filing (with potential penalty). Over-withholding means the employee gave an interest-free loan to the government. Employers aim for accuracy per the W-4. Tax professionals encourage employees to review withholding after life events: marriage, divorce, new child, job change, large investment income. IRS Withholding Estimator helps.
questions.highlight: Goal: Withholding ≈ Actual tax liability. Neither over- nor under-withhold.

questions.q: What is a nonresident alien's tax treaty benefit and how is it claimed?
questions.a: The US has tax treaties with 65+ countries that may reduce or eliminate withholding on wages, scholarships, or other income. NRA employees must file Form 8233 (exemption from withholding on compensation) claiming the treaty benefit, with supporting documentation. Valid for 1 year. Employer reviews and submits copy to IRS within 5 days. If treaty exemption exceeds wages, Form W-4 adjustment may also be needed. Tax treaties are employer-specific — change of employer requires new Form 8233.
questions.highlight: Form 8233: NRA's claim for treaty-based withholding exemption. Renew annually.

questions.q: What is the withholding for bonus payments?
questions.a: Bonuses and supplemental wages under $1 million cumulative can be withheld using the flat percentage method at 22%. Alternatively, use the aggregate method: combine the bonus with regular wages from most recent pay period (or average of prior periods), calculate withholding on the total, subtract regular withholding already done, withhold the balance. Employer can choose method. Flat 22% is simpler for KPO processing.
questions.highlight: Bonus withholding: Flat 22% OR aggregate method. Both are acceptable.

questions.q: What is employer-paid moving expense and its tax treatment post-TCJA?
questions.a: Pre-TCJA (Tax Cuts and Jobs Act), qualified employer-paid moving expense reimbursements were excludable from income. Post-TCJA (2018–2025): all employer-paid moving expense reimbursements (except for active military) are taxable wages included in W-2 Box 1 and subject to all employment taxes. Many employers 'gross up' moving reimbursements to cover the employee's additional tax burden. After TCJA sunsets in 2026, pre-TCJA rules would return unless extended.
questions.highlight: Post-TCJA moving reimbursements = fully taxable wages (except active military).

questions.q: What is a gross-up calculation in payroll?
questions.a: A gross-up is a calculation to determine the gross amount to pay so that after taxes, the employee nets a specific target amount. Formula: Gross = Net Target / (1 − Combined Tax Rate). Example: Target net = $1,000, combined rate = 40%. Gross = $1,000 / 0.60 = $1,666.67. Used for: relocation reimbursements, equity awards, prize winnings, or when employers want to cover the employee's tax burden. The gross-up amount itself is taxable, creating an iterative calculation.
questions.highlight: Gross-up = Net amount ÷ (1 − Total tax rate). Gross-ups are themselves taxable.

questions.q: What is Form 945 and when is it used?
questions.a: Form 945 (Annual Return of Withheld Federal Income Tax) reports federal income tax withheld from non-payroll payments including: 1099 backup withholding, pension/IRA withholding, military retirement, Indian gaming profits, and voluntary withholding on unemployment compensation. Filed annually by January 31. Separate from Form 941 (employment taxes). Deposits follow same rules as 941 deposits (EFTPS). Filed per EIN.
questions.highlight: Form 945 = Annual return for non-payroll federal withholding. Due January 31.

questions.q: What are the 2024 tax brackets for married filing jointly?
questions.highlight: MFJ brackets are roughly double single brackets through 32% — the marriage bonus.

questions.q: What is the earned income tax credit (EITC) and its payroll relevance?
questions.a: The EITC is a refundable tax credit for low-to-moderate income workers. 2024 maximum: $7,830 (3+ children). Payroll relevance: Employers must notify employees about potential EITC eligibility when issuing W-2s (IRS Notice 1015). Some states also have state EITC. EITC has no direct impact on payroll withholding calculations — it's claimed on Form 1040. However, employees who expect EITC may reduce withholding on W-4 to reflect anticipated refund.
questions.highlight: Employer obligation: Include EITC notification (IRS Notice 1015) with W-2 distribution.

questions.q: What is the kiddie tax and does it affect payroll withholding?
questions.a: The kiddie tax (IRC Section 1(g)) applies to children under 19 (under 24 if full-time student) who have unearned income over $2,500 — it's taxed at parents' marginal rate. Limited payroll relevance: if a minor is employed in the family business, their wages are subject to normal withholding rules. If the employer-child is under 18 and the parent employs them in a sole proprietorship or partnership, wages may be exempt from FICA (not applicable to corporations).
questions.highlight: Under-18 children employed by parent's sole proprietorship: exempt from FICA.

questions.q: What is voluntary withholding and how is it set up?
questions.a: Voluntary withholding allows recipients of non-wage payments to have federal income tax withheld voluntarily. Common situations: pension/retirement distributions (Form W-4P), unemployment compensation (Form W-4V), Social Security benefits (Form W-4V), gambling winnings, and certain agricultural payments. Rate options on W-4V: 7%, 10%, 12%, or 22%. No required withholding on pensions unless the payer chooses a default rate; recipients can opt out with Form W-4P.
questions.highlight: Form W-4V: Voluntary withholding on Social Security and unemployment. Rates: 7/10/12/22%.

questions.q: What is FIT (Federal Income Tax) vs. FWT (Federal Withholding Tax)?
questions.a: FIT and FWT are often used interchangeably in payroll — both refer to the federal income tax withheld from employee paychecks per the W-4 instructions and Pub 15-T tables. Technically, the broader category is employment taxes (FICA + FIT). FIT/FWT specifically refers to income tax withholding. The combined FIT + employee FICA + employer FICA is deposited together via EFTPS. On Form 941, Line 3 is federal income tax withheld (FIT).
questions.highlight: On Form 941: Line 3 = FIT withheld. Lines 5a-5d = FICA. All deposited together via EFTPS.

questions.q: What is the IRS's approach to underreporting by employers?
questions.a: The IRS uses several tools to detect employer under-withholding: CP2000 notices (mismatches between W-2/1099 reporting and returns), National Research Program audits, Information Document Requests (IDR), and tip-offs from employees. For willful failures, the IRS can assess civil penalties (FTD, FTP, FTF) or criminal penalties (tax evasion, filing false returns). Employment tax examination teams specifically audit payroll compliance. Regular internal reconciliation is the best defense.
questions.highlight: IRS uses W-2/941 mismatch analysis as primary tool to detect employer underreporting.

questions.q: What is the penalty for failure to file W-2s on time?
questions.a:
Penalty structure for late/incorrect W-2s (per form):
  • Filed ≤30 days late: $60/form (max $630K/year, $220K small employers)
  • Filed 31 days late to Aug 1: $120/form (max $1.891M/year)
  • Filed after Aug 1 or not at all: $310/form (max $3.783M/year)
  • Intentional disregard: $630/form, no cap
These penalties apply separately for failing to provide employee copies. E-filing required for 10+ forms.
questions.highlight: E-filing required when submitting 10+ W-2s. Paper filing threshold dropped from 250 to 10 in 2024.

questions.q: What is the payroll tax deferral and its implications?
questions.a: During COVID-19 (2020), employers could defer the employer's share of Social Security taxes (6.2%) from March 27–December 31, 2020, under the CARES Act. The deferred amounts had to be repaid by December 31, 2021 (50%) and December 31, 2022 (remaining 50%). In normal operations, there is no payroll tax deferral available to employers. Some self-employed individuals could defer self-employment taxes similarly. Lessons for KPO: tax deferrals create future liabilities that must be tracked.
questions.highlight: CARES Act deferral: 50% repaid by Dec 31, 2021; balance by Dec 31, 2022.

questions.q: What is FICA and what taxes does it include?
questions.a:
FICA (Federal Insurance Contributions Act) requires both employees and employers to contribute to two social insurance programs:
  • Social Security (OASDI): 6.2% employee + 6.2% employer = 12.4% total. Wage base: $168,600 (2024)
  • Medicare (HI): 1.45% employee + 1.45% employer = 2.9% total. No wage base cap
  • Additional Medicare Tax: 0.9% employee only on wages over $200K
questions.highlight: Total FICA rate: 15.3% (combined employee + employer). Self-employed pay full 15.3%.

questions.q: What is the Social Security wage base for 2024?
questions.a: The Social Security wage base (also called the OASDI wage base or SS taxable maximum) for 2024 is $168,600. This means the 6.2% Social Security tax applies only to the first $168,600 of wages per employee per year. Once an employee's cumulative wages reach this cap, Social Security withholding stops for the remainder of the year. The base increases annually based on national average wage indexing. In 2023 it was $160,200.
questions.highlight: 2024 SS Wage Base: $168,600. Once reached, SS withholding stops for that calendar year.

questions.q: Is there a wage base for Medicare taxes?
questions.a: No. Medicare tax applies to all wages with no wage base cap. The 1.45% employee portion and 1.45% employer portion apply to every dollar of wages earned throughout the year. The only change above normal wages is the Additional Medicare Tax: 0.9% on wages exceeding $200,000 (employer withholding threshold) — employer does not match this extra 0.9%. This makes Medicare the only employment tax that never stops for high-earning employees.
questions.highlight: Medicare has NO wage base cap — 2.9% applies to all wages at every income level.

questions.q: What wages are exempt from FICA?
questions.a:
FICA-exempt wages include:
  • Pre-tax cafeteria plan benefits (health premiums under Section 125)
  • Employer contributions to 401(k)/403(b) plans
  • Certain student workers at universities
  • Church employees who have filed Form 4361
  • Nonresident alien students (F, J, M, Q visas) in specific situations
  • Tips below $20/month
  • Certain railroad workers (covered by RRT instead)
questions.highlight: Section 125 pre-tax health premiums reduce FICA — both employee and employer save.

questions.q: What is self-employment tax and how does it relate to FICA?
questions.a: Self-employment (SE) tax is the equivalent of FICA for self-employed individuals. Rate: 15.3% on net self-employment income (12.4% SS + 2.9% Medicare), same as the combined employer + employee FICA. However: SE tax applies to 92.35% of net SE income (because employees' wages don't include the employer's share). Deduction: 50% of SE tax is deductible on Schedule 1 of Form 1040 (above-the-line). Reported on Schedule SE.
questions.highlight: SE tax = 15.3% × 92.35% of net SE income. Deduct 50% of SE tax on Schedule 1.

questions.q: What happens when an employee over-pays Social Security tax due to multiple employers?
questions.a: If an employee works for multiple employers in the same year and combined wages exceed the SS wage base ($168,600 in 2024), each employer independently withholds 6.2% on the first $168,600. The employee may end up with excess SS withheld. The excess is claimed as a credit on Form 1040 (refundable). Employers do NOT get a refund of their 6.2% match — each employer's FICA is independent. KPO teams advise clients on this for employees with multiple income sources.
questions.highlight: Excess SS withholding from multiple employers = refundable credit on employee's Form 1040.

questions.q: What are FICA tip credits?
questions.a: The FICA tip credit (IRC §45B) allows food and beverage employers to claim a tax credit for the employer's FICA share paid on employee tips above the minimum wage amount. Credit = 7.65% × (Tips − amount needed to bring employee to minimum wage). Helps offset the employer FICA cost on tips, which can be significant in the restaurant industry. Requires employee reporting of all tips. Claimed on Form 8846. KPO firms serving restaurant clients often handle this.
questions.highlight: IRC §45B FICA tip credit: 7.65% of tips above minimum wage equivalent, claimed on Form 8846.

questions.q: What is the Railroad Retirement Tax Act (RRTA)?
questions.a: The RRTA is a parallel system to FICA specifically for railroad workers. Instead of FICA, railroad employees and employers contribute to the Railroad Retirement Board (RRB). Tier 1 taxes (similar to FICA): 6.2%/6.2% for employees/employers on first $168,600 (2024). Tier 2 taxes: additional retirement contribution. Benefits are higher than Social Security. Railroad W-2 uses different boxes. KPO firms rarely encounter this but should know it exists.
questions.highlight: RRTA replaces FICA for railroad employees — separate system administered by RRB.

questions.q: How are tips treated for FICA purposes?
questions.a: Tips of $20 or more in a calendar month must be reported by employees to their employer by the 10th of the following month (Form 4070 or similar). Reported tips are subject to employee FICA (both SS and Medicare). Employer must also pay their 7.65% matching FICA on those tips. Unreported tips: employee still owes FICA but employer liability is limited. Allocated tips (W-2 Box 8) may be added to an employee's wages if actual tips seem understated based on service charges.
questions.highlight: Tips ≥ $20/month must be reported to employer. Both employee and employer owe FICA on tips.

questions.q: What is SECA and how is it different from FICA?
questions.a:
SECA (Self-Employment Contributions Act) governs self-employment taxes for the self-employed — it is the self-employed equivalent of FICA. Key differences:
  • SECA applies to Schedule C/F profit and partnership income
  • Net earnings × 92.35% is the tax base (employer share adjustment)
  • The individual pays both 'employee' and 'employer' portions
  • Deduction of 50% of SECA reduces income tax (but not SE tax)
  • Reported on Schedule SE, not on W-2
questions.highlight: SECA self-employment tax: 92.35% × Net Earnings × 15.3%. Schedule SE required.

questions.q: What is a Section 3121(b) exception?
questions.a: IRC Section 3121(b) defines 'employment' for FICA purposes and lists numerous exceptions. Notable FICA exemptions: (1) services of children under 18 in parent's unincorporated business; (2) services of students in school they attend (under certain conditions); (3) services of election workers who earn less than $2,000 in a year; (4) services performed by foreign government employees; (5) services by ordained ministers (who can opt out with Form 4361). These are specific carve-outs, not general rules.
questions.highlight: Section 3121(b): Lists FICA exemptions. Knowing these prevents over-withholding.

questions.q: What is the Medicare surtax on investment income?
questions.a: The Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) — also called the 3.8% Medicare surtax — applies to the lesser of net investment income (dividends, capital gains, rental income) or the amount by which MAGI exceeds $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ). This is NOT a payroll tax — it's not withheld from wages. It applies to investment income. However, high-income employees may need to increase W-4 withholding or make estimated tax payments to cover this. KPO must understand but not confuse with employment taxes.
questions.highlight: NIIT (3.8%): Investment income tax, NOT a payroll tax. Not withheld from wages.

questions.q: What is Form 941 used for?
questions.a: Form 941 (Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return) reports: federal income tax withheld from employees, employer and employee Social Security tax, employer and employee Medicare tax, and any applicable credits. Filed quarterly: April 30 (Q1), July 31 (Q2), October 31 (Q3), January 31 (Q4). If filed late: 5% penalty/month up to 25%. Must reconcile with EFTPS deposits. All employers (except agricultural and household) with employees file 941.
questions.highlight: Form 941 due dates: April 30, July 31, October 31, January 31 (10 days extra if deposits current).

questions.q: What is the FICA credit for small businesses?
questions.a: Under the Small Business Health Care Tax Credit (Form 8941), eligible small employers who pay at least 50% of employee health insurance premiums may claim a credit of up to 50% of premiums paid (35% for tax-exempt). Requirements: fewer than 25 FTE employees, average wages below $56,000 (2024), coverage through SHOP Marketplace. FICA taxes themselves don't generate a credit except the specific §45B FICA tip credit for restaurants. KPO should not confuse these two different credits.
questions.highlight: Small business health credit (Form 8941): up to 50% of premiums paid through SHOP.

questions.q: How are FICA taxes calculated for a semi-monthly payroll?
questions.a:
Example: Employee earns $60,000/year semi-monthly salary = $2,500/period. FICA per paycheck:
  • Social Security: $2,500 × 6.2% = $155.00
  • Medicare: $2,500 × 1.45% = $36.25
  • Total employee FICA: $191.25
  • Employer matches: $191.25 (until SS wage base)
When YTD wages hit $168,600, stop SS withholding. Medicare continues all year. Add 0.9% AMT when YTD hits $200K.
questions.highlight: SS stops at YTD $168,600. Medicare never stops. AMT 0.9% starts at YTD $200,000.

questions.q: What is a voluntary correction program for FICA errors?
questions.a: The IRS Voluntary Correction Program (VCP) for employment tax errors uses Form 941-X (Adjusted Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return). Employers can correct overreported or underreported taxes. Interest-free correction available if discovered within same year for some situations. For prior years: interest applies. Statute of limitations on assessment: 3 years from due date or filing of 941. Overcollected FICA: employer must repay employee or get employee's consent to treat as deduction.
questions.highlight: Form 941-X: Corrects previously filed Form 941. Must explain each correction.

questions.q: What does OASDI stand for in FICA?
questions.a: OASDI stands for Old Age, Survivors, and Disability Insurance — the formal name for the Social Security program funded by FICA. The Social Security Administration administers OASDI. When employees and employers pay the 6.2% Social Security portion of FICA, they fund: old-age benefits (retirement), survivors benefits (widow/widower), and disability insurance. This appears as 'OASDI' on pay stubs in many payroll systems.
questions.highlight: OASDI = Social Security. The 6.2% FICA SS tax funds retirement, survivors, and disability.

questions.q: What is the employee FICA rate and employer FICA rate?
questions.a: Employee FICA: SS 6.2% + Medicare 1.45% = 7.65% total. Employer FICA: SS 6.2% + Medicare 1.45% = 7.65% total — exactly matching the employee rate. Plus, employer must withhold the 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax once employee wages exceed $200K (but employer does NOT match this extra 0.9%). Total combined rate on wages up to the SS wage base: 15.3%. On wages above SS wage base: 2.9% (Medicare only, both sides).
questions.highlight: Employee FICA = 7.65%. Employer FICA = 7.65%. Combined: 15.3% (up to SS wage base).

questions.q: What is the Medicare HI tax?
questions.a: HI stands for Hospital Insurance — the formal name for the Medicare portion of FICA. The Medicare HI tax is 1.45% from employee + 1.45% from employer = 2.9% combined, applied to all wages with no wage base limit. It funds Part A (hospital insurance) of Medicare. The Additional Medicare Tax (0.9%) also funds HI but is employee-only and applies only above the $200K threshold. On pay stubs, shown as 'Med' or 'Medicare'.
questions.highlight: Medicare HI = 1.45% each side = 2.9% combined. No wage base cap. Funds Medicare Part A.

questions.q: What is the FICA exemption for non-immigrant visa holders?
questions.a:
Certain non-immigrant visa holders are exempt from FICA:
  • F-1 and J-1 students: FICA-exempt while in status as non-resident aliens (typically first 5 years for F-1, 2 years for J-1)
  • J-1 non-students (professors, researchers): Exempt for 2 years
  • Q-1 cultural exchange: Similar to J-1
Once NRA becomes resident alien for tax purposes (substantial presence test), FICA applies. H-1B and L-1 visa holders: fully subject to FICA.
questions.highlight: F-1/J-1 student NRAs: FICA exempt. H-1B/L-1 workers: fully subject to FICA.

questions.q: How are FICA taxes handled for domestic workers?
questions.a: Under the 'nanny tax' rules, household employers who pay a domestic employee (nanny, housekeeper, elder care worker) $2,700+ (2024 threshold) in cash wages must pay FICA. Both employer and employee share: 7.65% each. Household employers do NOT file Form 941 — instead, household employment taxes are reported annually on Schedule H (Form 1040) and paid with the owner's personal income tax return. May also owe FUTA. Nannies get W-2.
questions.highlight: Household employers: file Schedule H with Form 1040. Threshold: $2,700 in cash wages (2024).

questions.q: Are employer FICA contributions tax deductible?
questions.a: Yes. The employer's share of FICA taxes (7.65% of employee wages) is fully deductible as a business expense on the employer's federal income tax return. This is separate from what's withheld from employees — it's an additional cost to the business. For sole proprietors, FICA as an employer is embedded in self-employment tax; they deduct 50% of total SE tax as above-the-line deduction on Schedule 1.
questions.highlight: Employer FICA = 7.65% of wages = fully deductible business expense.

questions.q: What is the Social Security Administration's role in payroll?
questions.a: The SSA (Social Security Administration) receives W-2/W-3 filings from employers and uses the data to update employees' Social Security earnings records, which determine future retirement/disability/survivor benefits. Discrepancies between W-2 wages and SSA records can reduce employees' future benefits. SSA shares wage data with IRS. Employers must ensure correct SSNs on W-2s — mismatch triggers SSA no-match letters. KPO firms should verify all SSNs before W-2 processing.
questions.highlight: W-2 wages feed SSA records — correct SSN on W-2 directly affects future SS benefits.

questions.q: What is the sick pay exclusion from FICA?
questions.a:
Third-party sick pay from insurance companies may be FICA-exempt after a waiting period. Key rules:
  • Sick pay after 6 months of absence is FICA-exempt
  • Sick pay within first 6 months: FICA applies if employer-funded
  • Third-party insurer (not the employer) pays: employer FICA may be shifted to insurer
  • W-2 reporting: third-party payer may file W-2 or transfer obligation to employer
Governed by IRC §3121(a)(4) and Form 8922 for aggregate reporting.
questions.highlight: Third-party sick pay after 6-month absence = FICA exempt. Within 6 months = taxable.

questions.q: What is the FICA 'wage continuation plan' issue?
questions.a: Wage continuation plans replace lost wages due to disability. FICA treatment: If employer-funded, wages continue to be FICA-taxable for the first 6 months of absence. After 6 months (or any amount under purely employee-funded plans): FICA-exempt. Tax code Section 105 governs sick pay benefits from employer-funded plans. Employer must track start date of disability absence to correctly apply the FICA cutoff. Form 941 includes a specific line for third-party sick pay adjustments.
questions.highlight: Employer-funded sick/disability pay: FICA applies for first 6 months of absence.

questions.q: What are the FICA rates for 2024?
questions.a:
2024 FICA rates:
  • Social Security (OASDI): 6.2% employee + 6.2% employer. Wage base: $168,600
  • Medicare (HI): 1.45% employee + 1.45% employer. No wage base limit
  • Additional Medicare Tax: 0.9% employee only on wages over $200K (single threshold)
These rates have been stable for many years. The SS wage base adjusts annually with inflation. FICA rates can only be changed by Congress.

questions.q: What is the employer match requirement for FICA?
questions.a:
Every employer must match the employee's FICA contributions dollar-for-dollar:
  • Match Social Security: 6.2% × employee's wages (up to wage base)
  • Match Medicare: 1.45% × all employee wages
  • No match on Additional Medicare Tax (0.9%)
Employer match is NOT withheld from employee pay — it's an additional cost. Included in payroll tax deposits. Both employee and employer shares are deposited together via EFTPS. Employer match is a deductible business expense.
questions.highlight: Employer FICA match is mandatory and separate from employee withholding.

questions.q: What is a FICA refund claim and how is it made?
questions.a:
If an employer over-withholds FICA taxes, the correction process depends on timing:
  • Current year: Adjust on current 941 (Form 941 adjustment)
  • Prior year: File Form 941-X with explanation
For employee over-collections: employer must return overcollected amounts to employee and reduce deposit by equivalent amount. Cannot simply keep excess. Statute of limitations on refund claims: 3 years from due date of return or 2 years from date tax paid, whichever is later.
questions.highlight: Overcollected employee FICA must be refunded to employee before claiming employer refund.

questions.q: What is the 'safe harbor' rule for FICA on tips?
questions.a: Employers are not liable for employer FICA on tips that employees fail to report, as long as the employer has fulfilled its reporting obligations. However, the IRS may reallocate unreported tips based on examination. To protect themselves, restaurants can enter into a Tip Rate Determination Agreement (TRDA) or Tip Reporting Alternative Commitment (TRAC) with the IRS, specifying standard tip rates. This provides audit protection on tip FICA if the program requirements are met.
questions.highlight: TRAC/TRDA agreements protect employers from FICA liability on unreported employee tips.

questions.q: What is IRS Form 941 Schedule B?
questions.a: Schedule B (Report of Tax Liability for Semiweekly Schedule Depositors) must be attached to Form 941 by semi-weekly depositors. It shows the specific daily tax liability for each day of the quarter, which allows the IRS to verify that deposits were made on the correct schedule. Note: Schedule B reports the tax liability (when wages were paid), not when deposits were made. If Schedule B totals don't match Form 941 line 12, the IRS will notice and may assess penalties.
questions.highlight: Schedule B shows daily tax liability — required only for semi-weekly depositors with Form 941.

questions.q: What states have no state income tax?
questions.a: As of 2024, 9 states have no individual income tax: Alaska, Florida, Nevada, New Hampshire (taxes only interest/dividends, phasing out), South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Washington (no wage income tax, but has capital gains tax), and Wyoming. New Hampshire's dividend/interest tax was repealed effective Jan 1, 2025. This matters for KPO firms because employees in these states have no state income tax withholding, simplifying payroll but not eliminating FUTA/SUTA obligations.
questions.highlight: 9 no-income-tax states: AK, FL, NV, NH, SD, TN, TX, WA, WY (verify annually as laws change).

questions.q: What is SUTA (State Unemployment Tax Act)?
questions.a: SUTA is a state payroll tax paid entirely by employers (not withheld from employees, with minor exceptions in NJ, PA, AK) to fund state unemployment insurance. Rates vary by state and employer's experience rating (claims history). New employer rates: typically 1%–3.4%. Wage base varies: from $7,000 (FL) to $62,500 (WA) as of 2024. Unlike FUTA, SUTA rates reflect individual employer history. Better employment record = lower SUTA rate.
questions.highlight: SUTA: Employer-paid (generally), rates based on experience rating, varies dramatically by state.

questions.q: What is FUTA and how does SUTA credit work?
questions.a: FUTA (Federal Unemployment Tax Act) is a federal employer-paid payroll tax: 6% on first $7,000 of each employee's wages per year. However, employers who pay SUTA timely receive a credit of up to 5.4% (SUTA credit), reducing effective FUTA rate to 0.6% (or $42/employee/year max). States with federal loan balances (credit reduction states) lose part of this credit — called FUTA credit reduction. Employers in credit reduction states pay more than 0.6%.
questions.highlight: Normal FUTA = 6% × $7,000 = $420 per employee. Credit reduces to $42 effective (0.6% × $7,000).

questions.q: What is the FUTA credit reduction?
questions.a: When a state has borrowed from the federal government to pay unemployment benefits and hasn't repaid the loan by November 10, the state becomes a 'credit reduction state.' Employers in that state lose 0.3% of the 5.4% SUTA credit per year. Example: If California has had a 2-year loan, 0.6% credit reduction applies → effective FUTA rate = 6% − 5.4% + 0.6% = 1.2% on $7,000. Reported on Schedule A of Form 940. Listed annually by IRS in November.
questions.highlight: Credit reduction states: Each year of outstanding loan = 0.3% less FUTA credit for employers.

questions.q: What is Form 940 and when is it due?
questions.a: Form 940 (Employer's Annual Federal Unemployment Tax Return) reports an employer's FUTA tax. Filed annually, due January 31 (or February 10 if all FUTA taxes were deposited on time). FUTA deposits are quarterly if liability exceeds $500 — otherwise can pay annually with return. Form 940 is separate from Form 941. Attach Schedule A for multistate employers or credit reduction states. If total FUTA for year ≤$500, no deposit required; pay with Form 940.
questions.highlight: Form 940 due: January 31 annually (Feb 10 if deposits current). Deposits when liability > $500.

questions.q: What is an experience rating for SUTA?
questions.a: An experience rating (also called merit rating) determines an employer's SUTA tax rate based on their unemployment claims history. New employers start at the state's standard new-employer rate (typically 2%–3.5%). After 1–3 years, the rate adjusts annually based on: reserves-to-taxable-payroll ratio, benefit charges (former employees' unemployment claims), and state fund balance. Employers with few claims get lower rates; high turnover employers pay more. KPO firms typically maintain low experience rates.
questions.highlight: Lower unemployment claims = lower SUTA experience rate = significant employer savings.

questions.q: What is a reciprocal agreement between states?
questions.a: A reciprocal agreement (or reciprocity agreement) between two states allows employees who live in one state but work in another to pay income taxes only in their home state. 36+ states have such agreements. Example: An employee lives in New Jersey but works in Pennsylvania — under their reciprocal agreement, they only pay NJ taxes. Employer withholds for the employee's home state instead of work state. Employee files Form REV-419 (PA) or similar to claim exemption from work-state withholding.
questions.highlight: Reciprocal agreements = withhold for employee's HOME state, not work state.

questions.q: What is nexus and how does it affect state payroll tax obligations?
questions.a: Nexus is a sufficient connection between a business and a state that requires the business to comply with that state's tax laws (including payroll). For payroll, nexus is typically created when an employee physically works in a state. Post-COVID remote work has created nexus challenges: if an employee moves to a different state and works remotely, the employer may need to register in that state, withhold that state's income tax, pay SUTA there, and register with the state labor department.
questions.highlight: Remote workers = potential new state nexus. Must register, withhold, and pay SUTA in employee's work state.

questions.q: What is New York State's 'convenience of employer' rule?
questions.a: New York (and a few other states like Pennsylvania, Nebraska, Delaware) have a 'convenience of employer' rule that taxes non-residents on days they could have worked in NY but chose to work from home for their own convenience. If a NY employer allows an employee to work from another state for their own convenience (not required by employer), NY may tax those wages as NY income. This creates potential double taxation for remote workers in NY-employer situations.
questions.highlight: NY convenience rule: working remotely from outside NY can still trigger NY income tax.

questions.q: What is the unemployment insurance application process for a new employer?
questions.a: New employers must register with each state where they have employees within the required timeframe (often within 30 days of hiring first employee or paying $1,500+ in wages).
