401(k) plans must pass two main nondiscrimination tests:
  • ADP test: Actual Deferral Percentage — HCE deferral rate cannot exceed non-HCE rate by more than 2% or 1.25× (whichever provides more). HCE = $155K+ salary (2024) or 5%+ owner
  • ACP test: Actual Contribution Percentage — tests employer match
Failure: return excess contributions to HCEs or make QNECs to NHCEs. Safe harbor plans automatically pass. KPO teams help clients calculate nondiscrimination testing.
questions.highlight: HCE threshold (2024): $155,000 prior year compensation OR 5%+ owner. ADP/ACP tests annual.

questions.q: What is group term life insurance imputed income?
questions.a: Employer-provided group term life insurance coverage over $50,000 creates imputed income under IRS Table I. The cost of coverage is determined using IRS uniform premium rates based on age brackets (5-year age bands). Example: For an employee age 47 with $200,000 coverage: imputed income = ($200,000 − $50,000) / $1,000 × monthly rate × 12 months. This imputed amount is included in W-2 Box 1 and Box 12 Code C, and subject to FICA. Not subject to income tax withholding by employer.
questions.highlight: GTL over $50K: Imputed income = IRS Table I rates by age bracket. W-2 Box 12 Code C.

questions.q: What is the personal use of company vehicle imputed income calculation?
questions.a:
When employees use company vehicles for personal purposes, the FMV of that use is imputed income. Three valuation methods:
  • General Valuation: FMV of the lease value (requires appraisal)
  • Annual Lease Value Method: IRS table based on FMV of vehicle at first availability × personal use %
  • Cents-per-Mile Method: Personal miles × IRS mileage rate (67¢ in 2024; must meet annual mileage requirements)
  • Commuting Rule: $1.50/one-way commute trip (specific conditions)
Imputed income = income tax + FICA.
questions.highlight: Company car personal use: Imputed income calculated by one of 4 IRS-approved methods.

questions.q: What is a Section 132 fringe benefit?
questions.a:
IRC Section 132 lists specific fringe benefits excluded from employees' income:
  • No-additional-cost services (airline seats, hotel rooms)
  • Qualified employee discounts (up to 20% for services)
  • Working condition fringe (business tools, safety equipment)
  • De minimis fringe (coffee, occasional meals, holiday gifts under ~$100)
  • Qualified transportation ($315/month transit/parking in 2024)
  • On-premises athletic facilities
  • Qualified moving expense (military only post-TCJA)
questions.highlight: Section 132 de minimis: Small, infrequent benefits (coffee, holiday gift <$100) are non-taxable.

questions.q: What is a qualified transportation fringe benefit?
questions.a:
Qualified transportation benefits (Section 132(f)):
  • Transit passes/vanpool benefits: Up to $315/month (2024), excludable from income and FICA
  • Qualified parking: Up to $315/month (2024), excludable
  • Qualified bicycle commuting: Was $20/month before TCJA suspended it (2018–2025)
Employer can provide directly or through salary reduction (Section 125). Employees can also reduce salary pre-tax for transit/parking. Amounts above limits are taxable. Not deductible by employer as business expense since TCJA.
questions.highlight: 2024 Transit/Parking: $315/month each. Pre-tax reduces income + FICA. Employer can't deduct.

questions.q: What is a cafeteria plan and what benefits can it include?
questions.a: A Section 125 cafeteria plan allows employees to choose between taxable cash compensation and non-taxable benefits, reducing taxes for both. Eligible benefits: health insurance premiums, FSA (healthcare and dependent care), HSA contributions, group term life, accident/disability insurance. Cannot include: scholarships, meals/lodging (Section 119), qualified transportation (separately excluded), athletic facilities, NQDC plans. Must have a written plan document. Cannot discriminate in favor of key employees. Simple cafeteria plan option available for small employers.
questions.highlight: Section 125: Employees pick benefits with pre-tax dollars. Must have written plan document.

questions.q: What is a federal garnishment and how is it administered?
questions.a: Federal (and state) garnishments are court orders requiring employers to deduct amounts from employee wages. Types: child support (highest priority), student loans (Department of Education), tax levy (IRS Form 668-W), creditor garnishments. Process: employer receives court order/IRS notice → determines disposable earnings → calculates maximum allowable withholding → deducts per schedule → remits to designated agency. Employer may charge small admin fee (varies by state). Cannot fire employee for first garnishment (CCPA protection).
questions.highlight: CCPA: Cannot fire employee for first garnishment. Multi-garnishment situations are complex.

questions.q: What is an IRS Form 668-W (tax levy) and how is it handled in payroll?
questions.a: IRS Form 668-W (Notice of Levy on Wages, Salary, and Other Income) requires the employer to withhold wages for unpaid federal taxes. Employee completes Part 3 (Statement of Exemptions); employer calculates exempt amount based on filing status and dependents using Publication 1494 tables. Levy amount = net pay − exempt amount. Must begin withholding on first paycheck after receiving levy. Continue until IRS releases (Form 668-D) or debt paid. Cannot ignore — employer becomes liable for amounts not withheld.
questions.highlight: Form 668-W: Federal tax levy. Exempt amount per Pub 1494 table. Cannot stop without IRS release.

questions.q: What is child support income withholding order (IWO)?
questions.a: An IWO is a standardized court order (OMB Form 0970-0154) directing employers to withhold child support from an employee's wages and remit to the state disbursement unit. Federal law requires employers to begin withholding within the first pay period after receiving IWO. Withholding limits: CCPA allows up to 50% of disposable earnings for support (60% if not currently supporting another family), plus 5% if arrears exceed 12 weeks. Employer must remit within 7 business days (federal) or 2–3 days (state) of payday.
questions.highlight: IWO: Must begin withholding within first pay period. Remit within 7 business days of payday.

questions.q: What is a 403(b) retirement plan?
questions.a: A 403(b) plan (tax-sheltered annuity) is similar to a 401(k) but for employees of public schools, nonprofit organizations (501(c)(3)), and certain ministers. Same 2024 contribution limits: $23,000 employee deferral; $69,000 total. Pre-tax traditional or Roth option. Special catch-up: employees with 15+ years of service at certain organizations can contribute extra $3,000/year (lifetime $15,000 max) on top of age-50 catch-up. Payroll processing: very similar to 401(k). W-2 Box 12 Code E for 403(b) deferrals.
questions.highlight: 403(b): Like 401(k) for nonprofits/schools. Same limits. Box 12 Code E. Special 15-year catch-up.

questions.q: What is a 457(b) deferred compensation plan?
questions.a: 457(b) is a deferred compensation plan for state/local government employees and certain tax-exempt organizations. Unlike 401(k)/403(b), there's no 10% early withdrawal penalty — can access funds at separation from service regardless of age. Same contribution limit: $23,000 (2024). Special catch-up: in last 3 years before normal retirement age, can contribute double ($46,000). Pre-tax or Roth. Governments can also offer 457(f) (top-hat) plans for key executives with different rules. W-2 Box 12 Code G.
questions.highlight: 457(b): No 10% early withdrawal penalty. Government employees. W-2 Box 12 Code G.

questions.q: What is the 415 annual additions limit for retirement plans?
questions.a: IRC Section 415 limits the total annual additions (employee deferrals + employer contributions + forfeitures) to defined contribution plans: $69,000 for 2024 ($76,500 with catch-up for age 50+). This is the absolute ceiling regardless of plan type. For defined benefit plans, the maximum annual benefit is the lesser of $275,000 or 100% of participant's average compensation for 3 highest years. When an employee participates in multiple plans at the same employer, the 415 limit applies in the aggregate. Excess contributions must be returned.
questions.highlight: Section 415 DC limit: $69,000 total annual additions (2024). Includes employer + employee + forfeitures.

questions.q: What is a 401(k) catch-up contribution?
questions.a: Employees age 50 or older can make additional 'catch-up' contributions to their 401(k) or 403(b) beyond the standard limit. 2024 catch-up: $7,500, bringing total employee deferral to $30,500. SECURE 2.0 Act (2022) created a new 'super catch-up' for ages 60–63: $11,250 additional (effective 2025), increasing maximum to $34,750. Payroll systems must accommodate age-based limits and verify employee age before allowing catch-up contributions. Roth catch-up requirement for HCEs added by SECURE 2.0 (guidance pending).
questions.highlight: Age 50+ catch-up: +$7,500 (2024). SECURE 2.0 super catch-up ages 60-63: +$11,250 (2025).

questions.q: What is a SIMPLE IRA and how is it processed in payroll?
questions.a: A SIMPLE (Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees) IRA is a retirement plan for employers with 100 or fewer employees. Employee deferral limit: $16,000 (2024); catch-up +$3,500 (age 50+). Employer must contribute either 3% matching (100% of employee contribution) or 2% non-elective for all eligible employees. Payroll processing: pre-tax deductions like 401(k). W-2 Box 12 Code S (employee deferrals). Box 13 retirement plan box checked. Contributions remitted no later than 30 days after month end.
questions.highlight: SIMPLE IRA: For ≤100 employee firms. Limit $16,000. Employer must match 3% or contribute 2%.

questions.q: What is a SEP-IRA in terms of payroll/accounting?
questions.a: A SEP (Simplified Employee Pension) IRA is an employer-funded retirement plan. Only employers contribute (employees cannot make elective deferrals). Contribution limit: 25% of compensation up to $69,000 (2024). Must contribute equal % for all eligible employees. Extremely simple to administer — no 5500, no nondiscrimination testing, no written plan required. Tax deductible for employer. For sole proprietors: 20% of net SE income (after SE tax deduction). Payroll relevance: employer contribution is a payroll-adjacent expense, not withheld from employee wages.
questions.highlight: SEP-IRA: Employer-only contributions. Up to 25% of comp or $69,000. Minimal administration.

questions.q: What are the top-hat plan rules for executives?
questions.a: A top-hat plan is an unfunded, unsecured nonqualified deferred compensation plan maintained primarily for a select group of management or highly compensated employees. Exempt from most ERISA requirements (funding, vesting, claims, fiduciary rules) but must file a one-time statement with DOL and comply with IRC 409A. Payroll implications: deferred amounts are not reported on W-2 at deferral; FICA applies when amounts vest (special timing rule); distributions are ordinary income with FIT withholding and reported in W-2 Box 11 and Box 12 Code Z (if 409A violation).
questions.highlight: Top-hat plan: NQDC for executives. FICA at vesting; income tax at distribution. Box 11 W-2 reporting.

questions.q: What is double-entry bookkeeping?
questions.a: Double-entry bookkeeping is the accounting system where every transaction is recorded in at least two accounts — one account is debited and another is credited by the same amount. Based on the accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Each transaction maintains the balance. Example: pay employee wages → Debit Wage Expense $5,000 / Credit Cash $5,000. Developed by Luca Pacioli in 1494. Helps detect errors (books must balance) and provides complete transaction record.
questions.highlight: Double-entry: Every debit has an equal credit. Assets = Liabilities + Owner's Equity always.

questions.q: What is the chart of accounts?
questions.a: A chart of accounts (COA) is a numbered list of all account names used by an organization to record financial transactions. Structured into: Assets (1000s), Liabilities (2000s), Equity (3000s), Revenue (4000s), Expenses (5000s). Each transaction maps to specific account codes. The COA is the backbone of the general ledger. Well-designed COA enables meaningful financial reporting. Payroll-related accounts: Salaries Expense, Wages Expense, Payroll Tax Expense, Accrued Wages Payable, Payroll Tax Payable, 401(k) Payable.
questions.highlight: COA structure: 1000s = Assets, 2000s = Liabilities, 3000s = Equity, 4000s = Revenue, 5000s = Expense.

questions.q: What is the difference between cash basis and accrual accounting?
questions.a: Cash basis: Revenue recorded when cash is received; expenses recorded when cash is paid. Simpler, used by small businesses and individuals. Does not match revenues to periods. Accrual basis: Revenue recorded when earned; expenses recorded when incurred, regardless of cash movement. Required by GAAP for most businesses. More accurate for financial reporting. Example: December wages paid January 5 → Cash basis: January expense. Accrual basis: December expense (accrue wages payable in December).
questions.highlight: Accrual basis matches revenues to expenses in the period incurred — required by GAAP.

questions.q: What is a general ledger?
questions.a: The general ledger (GL) is the master record of all financial transactions of a business, organized by account. Contains every debit and credit entry for all accounts. The source for financial statements. Trial balance is generated from GL. Each GL account shows: date, description, journal entry reference, debit, credit, and running balance. Modern accounting systems (QuickBooks, NetSuite, SAP) maintain GL electronically. The GL must balance — total debits = total credits. Payroll journal entries post to specific GL accounts.
questions.highlight: General ledger = master record of all accounts. Trial balance pulls from GL to verify balance.

questions.q: What is a trial balance?
questions.a:
A trial balance is a statement listing all general ledger accounts with their debit or credit balances at a specific point in time. Purpose: verify that total debits equal total credits (double-entry check). Does NOT guarantee errors are absent — it only confirms the mathematical balance. Types:
  • Unadjusted trial balance: Before adjusting entries
  • Adjusted trial balance: After month-end adjusting entries
  • Post-closing trial balance: After closing entries
Basis for preparing financial statements.
questions.highlight: Trial balance: Debits must equal credits. Doesn't catch all errors (offsetting, wrong account, etc.).

questions.q: What are the three main financial statements?
questions.a:
• Income Statement (P&L): Shows revenues, expenses, and net income/loss for a period. Performance statement
  • Balance Sheet: Shows assets, liabilities, and equity at a point in time. Position statement. Assets = Liabilities + Equity
  • Cash Flow Statement: Shows cash inflows and outflows in operating, investing, and financing activities
Additional: Statement of Changes in Equity. All three connect — net income flows to equity on balance sheet; balance sheet changes appear on cash flow statement.
questions.highlight: Income Statement → Balance Sheet (via net income to retained earnings). Three statements interconnect.

questions.q: What is bank reconciliation and how is it done?
questions.a:
Bank reconciliation compares the company's book balance to the bank statement balance to identify and explain differences. Steps:
  • Start with bank balance
  • Add deposits in transit (sent to bank, not yet on statement)
  • Subtract outstanding checks (issued, not yet cleared)
  • Adjust for bank errors → Adjusted bank balance
  • Start with book balance
  • Add bank credits not in books (interest earned)
  • Subtract bank charges, NSF checks, service fees
  • Adjust for book errors → Must equal adjusted bank balance
questions.highlight: Adjusted Bank Balance MUST equal Adjusted Book Balance. Unexplained differences = errors or fraud.

questions.q: What is accounts payable and how is it managed?
questions.a: Accounts payable (AP) is money owed by a business to its suppliers and vendors for goods/services received but not yet paid. Current liability on balance sheet. AP management: receive invoice → verify against PO and receiving report (3-way match) → enter in AP system → approve for payment → pay by due date to capture discounts and avoid penalties. KPO firms often manage AP processing: coding invoices to proper GL accounts, handling approvals, processing payments, and reconciling vendor statements.
questions.highlight: 3-way match: Purchase Order + Receiving Report + Vendor Invoice = AP approval.

questions.q: What is accounts receivable and how does it affect bookkeeping?
questions.a: Accounts receivable (AR) is money owed to a business by customers for goods/services delivered but not yet paid. Current asset on balance sheet. Process: Invoice customer → track payment due dates → post payments when received → identify overdue accounts → calculate allowance for doubtful accounts. AR turnover ratio = Net Credit Sales / Average AR. Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) = AR / Daily Sales. Bad debt expense: Debit Bad Debt Expense / Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (balance sheet contra-asset).
questions.highlight: AR turnover ratio and DSO measure collection efficiency. Higher turnover = faster collection.

questions.q: What is the difference between debit and credit in accounting?
questions.a:
In accounting (not banking):
  • Debits INCREASE: Assets, Expenses, Dividends. DECREASE: Liabilities, Equity, Revenue
  • Credits INCREASE: Liabilities, Equity, Revenue. DECREASE: Assets, Expenses, Dividends
Memory aid: DEAD/CLIC — Debits Increase: Expenses, Assets, Dividends. Credits Increase: Liabilities, Income/Revenue, Contributions/Equity. This is counterintuitive to banking where 'credit' means more money in your account.
questions.highlight: DEAD/CLIC: Debits: Expenses/Assets/Dividends increase. Credits: Liabilities/Income/Equity increase.

questions.q: What is an adjusting journal entry?
questions.a:
Adjusting entries are made at period-end to ensure revenues and expenses are recorded in the correct period (accrual principle). Types:
  • Accruals: Record revenue/expense before cash changes (accrued wages, unbilled revenue)
  • Deferrals: Adjust previously recorded items (prepaid expenses, unearned revenue)
  • Depreciation: Allocate fixed asset cost over useful life
  • Amortization: Allocate intangible asset cost
  • Bad debt: Estimate uncollectible AR
Adjusting entries never involve cash.
questions.highlight: Adjusting entries: Never cash. Must be made before financial statements. Key to accrual accounting.

questions.q: What is depreciation and how is it recorded?
questions.a: Depreciation is the systematic allocation of a tangible fixed asset's cost over its useful life. It's a non-cash expense representing the using up of an asset. Journal entry: Debit Depreciation Expense / Credit Accumulated Depreciation (a contra-asset). Methods: Straight-line (most common for GAAP — cost minus salvage divided by useful life per year), Double-declining balance (accelerated), Sum-of-years digits (accelerated), Units of production. Book value = Cost − Accumulated Depreciation. Salvage value is not depreciated.
questions.highlight: Straight-line depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life years.

questions.q: What is the difference between capital expenditures and operating expenses?
questions.a: Capital expenditures (CapEx): Purchases of long-term assets (property, equipment, software) that benefit future periods. Capitalized on balance sheet, then depreciated/amortized. Not immediately expensed. Operating expenses (OpEx): Day-to-day costs of running the business (rent, utilities, salaries, supplies). Expensed immediately in the income statement. The distinction is crucial: capitalizing OpEx inflates assets and income; expensing CapEx reduces income immediately. IRS has specific capitalization rules (Section 263(a)) and de minimis safe harbor thresholds.
questions.highlight: CapEx: Balance sheet → depreciated over time. OpEx: Income statement → expensed immediately.

questions.q: What is inventory accounting?
questions.a: Inventory is goods held for sale or production materials. On balance sheet as current asset. Three inventory stages: Raw materials → Work in Process (WIP) → Finished Goods. Valuation methods: FIFO (First In, First Out — lower COGS in rising prices), LIFO (Last In, First Out — higher COGS in rising prices; not allowed under IFRS), Weighted Average Cost (blends all costs). Ending inventory = Beginning + Purchases − COGS. Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value (LCNRV) rule applies for GAAP.
questions.highlight: FIFO vs LIFO: In rising prices, FIFO → lower COGS + higher profit. LIFO → higher COGS + lower tax.

questions.q: What is petty cash and how is it managed?
questions.a: Petty cash is a small cash fund used for minor business expenses (postage, coffee, small supplies). Setup: Debit Petty Cash / Credit Cash for establishment amount. Replenishment (NOT establishment): Debit individual expense accounts / Credit Cash (based on receipts). At replenishment, the Petty Cash account itself is not debited — it stays the same. Year-end: physical count. Shortage: Debit Cash Short and Over (expense). Surplus: Credit Cash Short and Over (income). Custodian must reconcile regularly. Not usually material but important for internal controls.
questions.highlight: Petty cash replenishment: Debit Expenses / Credit Cash. Petty Cash account only changes if level changes.

questions.q: What is the allowance method for bad debts?
questions.a: The allowance method is required by GAAP for businesses with significant credit sales. Year-end estimate: Debit Bad Debt Expense / Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts. Methods to estimate: % of sales (income statement approach) or % of AR/aging schedule (balance sheet approach). Write-off of specific account: Debit Allowance / Credit AR (no new expense). Recovery of written-off account: Reverse write-off (Debit AR / Credit Allowance), then record payment (Debit Cash / Credit AR). Direct write-off method is simpler but not GAAP.
questions.highlight: Allowance method: Bad debt expense recognized when revenue recorded. Write-off hits allowance, not expense.

questions.q: What is a journal entry for payroll?
questions.a:
A complete payroll journal entry:
  • Debit: Salaries/Wages Expense (gross pay)
  • Credit: Federal Income Tax Payable (withheld)
  • Credit: State Income Tax Payable (withheld)
  • Credit: FICA — Social Security Payable (employee share)
  • Credit: FICA — Medicare Payable (employee share)
  • Credit: Health Insurance Payable (employee share)
  • Credit: 401(k) Payable (employee deferral)
  • Credit: Cash/Payroll Checking (net pay)
Separate entry for employer FICA: Debit Payroll Tax Expense / Credit FICA Payable.
questions.highlight: Payroll JE: Debit Gross Wages. Credit all withholdings + payroll liabilities + net pay (cash).

questions.q: What is the matching principle in accounting?
questions.a: The matching principle (GAAP) requires that expenses be recognized in the same period as the revenues they help generate. It's why: (1) wages earned in December must be accrued in December even if paid in January; (2) equipment must be depreciated over its useful life (not expensed all at once); (3) prepaid expenses are deferred and expensed as used. The matching principle is a cornerstone of accrual accounting and ensures accurate period reporting. Without it, expenses and revenues are misaligned, distorting profitability.
questions.highlight: Matching principle: Expenses recognized in same period as related revenue. Foundation of accrual accounting.

questions.q: What is accrued payroll and how is it recorded?
questions.a: Accrued payroll represents wages earned by employees but not yet paid at the end of an accounting period. Example: Pay period ends Dec 28, pay day is Jan 4. Wages for Dec 28–31 must be accrued. Journal entry (Dec 31): Debit Wages Expense / Credit Accrued Wages Payable. Reversal on Jan 1 (optional): Debit Accrued Wages Payable / Credit Wages Expense. When paid Jan 4: Debit Wages Payable / Credit Cash. Accrued wages appear as current liabilities on the balance sheet.
questions.highlight: Accrued wages: Debit Expense / Credit Wages Payable at period-end. Reversal simplifies next period.

questions.q: What is the difference between a journal and a ledger?
questions.a: A journal is the book of original entry — transactions are recorded chronologically as they occur with both debit and credit, a date, and a description. Each entry is self-contained showing both sides. A ledger (general ledger) is the book of final entry — transactions from journals are posted to individual account pages in the ledger, showing all activity and running balance for each account. Process: Source document → Journal (journalize) → Ledger (post) → Trial Balance → Financial Statements.
questions.highlight: Journal = chronological entries. Ledger = by account. Post from journal to ledger.

questions.q: What is a subsidiary ledger?
questions.a:
A subsidiary ledger is a detailed breakdown of a control account in the general ledger. Examples:
  • Accounts Receivable subsidiary ledger: One page per customer showing individual balances
  • Accounts Payable subsidiary ledger: One page per vendor
  • Inventory subsidiary ledger: One line per SKU
  • Fixed Asset subsidiary ledger: One record per asset
The sum of all subsidiary ledger balances must equal the control account in the GL. This allows detailed tracking without cluttering the GL with thousands of entries.
questions.highlight: Subsidiary ledger total must always equal the GL control account balance.

questions.q: What are closing entries and why are they necessary?
questions.a: Closing entries transfer temporary account balances (revenue, expense, dividend accounts) to the permanent equity account (Retained Earnings) at year-end. Process: (1) Close revenue accounts: Debit Revenue / Credit Income Summary; (2) Close expense accounts: Debit Income Summary / Credit Expenses; (3) Close Income Summary to Retained Earnings: Debit/Credit Income Summary / Credit/Debit Retained Earnings; (4) Close Dividends: Debit Retained Earnings / Credit Dividends. After closing, temporary accounts are zeroed out for the new period.
questions.highlight: Closing entries zero out temporary accounts (revenue/expenses) and update Retained Earnings.

questions.q: What is working capital and why is it important?
questions.a: Working capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Measures short-term liquidity. Positive working capital means the company can meet short-term obligations. Positive is generally good; negative may indicate trouble. Current ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities (healthy: 1.5–2.0). Quick ratio (acid test) = (Cash + Marketable Securities + AR) / Current Liabilities (excludes inventory). Payroll relevance: Accrued wages payable and payroll tax payable are current liabilities that reduce working capital.
questions.highlight: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Quick ratio excludes inventory (acid test).

questions.q: What is the revenue recognition principle?
questions.a: Revenue is recognized when it is earned and realizable — not necessarily when cash is received. GAAP's ASC 606 (effective for most companies by 2018–2019) establishes a 5-step model: (1) Identify the contract; (2) Identify performance obligations; (3) Determine transaction price; (4) Allocate transaction price to obligations; (5) Recognize revenue when/as obligation is satisfied. For payroll service companies, revenue = recognized per pay run processed or per period of service.
questions.highlight: ASC 606 5-step model: Identify contract → Obligations → Price → Allocate → Recognize.

questions.q: What is a contra account?
questions.a:
A contra account has a normal balance opposite to its related account, used to offset the parent account for net reporting. Examples:
  • Accumulated Depreciation (contra fixed asset): Normal credit balance; reduces asset book value
  • Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (contra AR): Normal credit balance; reduces AR to net realizable value
  • Sales Returns and Allowances (contra revenue): Normal debit balance; reduces gross sales
  • Treasury Stock (contra equity): Normal debit balance; reduces total equity
questions.highlight: Contra accounts offset related accounts. Accumulated Depreciation is the most common example.

questions.q: What is a prepaid expense and how is it treated?
questions.a: A prepaid expense is a payment made in advance for a benefit not yet received. Initially recorded as an asset (Debit Prepaid Expense / Credit Cash). As the benefit is used, adjusting entries expense it (Debit Expense / Credit Prepaid Expense). Examples: prepaid insurance (one year policy paid upfront), prepaid rent, prepaid subscriptions. At any balance sheet date, the remaining unexpired portion stays as an asset. Full expensing at time of payment would violate the matching principle.
questions.highlight: Prepaid expense: Pay now, asset first, expense as period passes. Debit Prepaid → Debit Expense over time.

questions.q: What is a deferred revenue?
questions.a: Deferred revenue (also called unearned revenue) is cash received before the service is performed or goods delivered. A current liability on balance sheet — the company owes the customer the service. Example: annual software subscription paid upfront. Journal entry at receipt: Debit Cash / Credit Deferred Revenue. Monthly recognition: Debit Deferred Revenue / Credit Service Revenue. At year-end, if service not fully delivered, remaining deferred balance stays on balance sheet. Very common in SaaS companies and subscription businesses.
questions.highlight: Deferred revenue = liability (cash received, service not yet delivered). Revenue recognized over time.

questions.q: What are fixed vs. variable costs?
questions.a: Fixed costs stay constant regardless of production/sales volume: rent, salaries (mostly), depreciation, insurance. Variable costs change proportionally with output: direct materials, commissions, shipping, direct labor (in manufacturing). Mixed (semi-variable) costs have both fixed and variable components: utilities, phone bills. This distinction is critical for: break-even analysis (BEP = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin per unit), budgeting, and operational decisions. Most payroll costs are semi-variable (base salary = fixed; overtime and bonuses = variable).
questions.highlight: Break-even point = Fixed Costs ÷ (Price − Variable Cost per unit).

questions.q: What is the cost of goods sold (COGS)?
questions.a: COGS is the direct cost of producing/purchasing goods sold during a period. Formula: Beginning Inventory + Purchases (or Production Costs) − Ending Inventory = COGS. Includes: direct materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead. Does NOT include: selling expenses, administrative expenses (these are period costs). Gross profit = Revenue − COGS. Gross margin % = Gross Profit / Revenue. For service businesses: no traditional COGS — instead 'cost of services' or 'direct costs'. Inventory method (FIFO/LIFO) directly impacts COGS.
questions.highlight: COGS = Beginning Inventory + Net Purchases − Ending Inventory. Gross Profit = Revenue − COGS.

questions.q: What is the income statement format?
questions.a: Standard income statement format: Revenue/Net Sales − COGS = Gross Profit − Operating Expenses (SG&A, R&D, Depreciation) = Operating Income (EBIT) +/− Non-Operating Items (interest, investment gains/losses) = Pre-tax Income − Income Tax Expense = Net Income. Key metrics: Gross Margin % (Gross Profit / Revenue), Operating Margin % (Operating Income / Revenue), Net Profit Margin % (Net Income / Revenue). EBITDA = EBIT + Depreciation + Amortization (proxy for operating cash flow).
questions.highlight: EBITDA = Operating Income + D&A. Common metric for business valuation and loan covenants.

questions.q: What is a balance sheet?
questions.a:
The balance sheet reports assets, liabilities, and equity at a specific date. Structure:
  • Assets: Current (cash, AR, inventory) + Non-current (PP&E, intangibles, investments)
  • Liabilities: Current (AP, accrued wages, current debt) + Non-current (long-term debt, deferred tax)
  • Equity: Common stock + Additional paid-in capital + Retained earnings − Treasury stock
Golden rule: Assets = Liabilities + Equity (always). Also called the Statement of Financial Position under IFRS.
questions.highlight: Balance sheet: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Payroll creates liabilities (wages payable, tax payable).

questions.q: What is the cash flow statement and its three sections?
questions.a:
The cash flow statement shows how cash moved during a period:
  • Operating Activities: Cash from core business operations. Start with net income, adjust for non-cash items (depreciation) and working capital changes. Most important section
  • Investing Activities: Cash for long-term asset purchases/sales, investments
  • Financing Activities: Cash from/for debt, equity, dividends
Two methods for operating section: Direct (actual cash flows) or Indirect (start with net income, reconcile). GAAP allows either; IFRS prefers direct. Payroll is in operating activities.
questions.highlight: Cash flow statement: Operating + Investing + Financing. Payroll is in Operating Activities.

questions.q: What is the difference between income and cash flow?
questions.a: Net income (accrual basis) and cash flow can differ significantly due to: (1) non-cash revenues/expenses (depreciation, amortization, stock-based comp); (2) timing differences (AR not yet collected = revenue recognized but no cash; AP not paid = expense incurred but cash not spent). A profitable company can have negative cash flow (over-investing in growth). A company can have positive cash flow while reporting losses (depreciation adds back). The cash flow statement bridges this gap. KPO accountants must explain this difference to clients.
questions.highlight: Profitable company can run out of cash. Cash-flow-positive company can report a loss. Both are possible.

questions.q: What is EBITDA and how is it calculated?
questions.a: EBITDA = Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. Calculated: Net Income + Interest Expense + Tax Expense + Depreciation + Amortization. Used as a proxy for operating cash flow and for comparing companies across different capital structures and tax environments. Commonly used in business valuation (enterprise value / EBITDA multiples), loan covenants, and M&A. Limitation: EBITDA ignores working capital, CapEx requirements, and is sometimes called 'earnings before bad stuff.' GAAP doesn't define EBITDA — non-GAAP metric.
questions.highlight: EBITDA = Net Income + Interest + Taxes + Depreciation + Amortization. Valuation metric.

questions.q: What is deferred tax liability and asset?
questions.a: Deferred taxes arise from temporary differences between book income (GAAP) and taxable income (tax code). Deferred Tax Liability (DTL): Tax will be paid later — book income > taxable income now (e.g., accelerated depreciation for tax). Debit Deferred Tax Expense / Credit DTL. Deferred Tax Asset (DTA): Tax already paid or will be saved — taxable income > book income now (e.g., warranties expensed for tax when paid). DTAs may require valuation allowance if unlikely to be realized. DTL is a non-current liability; DTA is a non-current asset.
questions.highlight: DTL: Pay less tax now, more later (accelerated depreciation). DTA: Pay more tax now, save later.

questions.q: What is inventory shrinkage and how is it recorded?
questions.a: Inventory shrinkage is the loss of inventory from theft, damage, miscounting, or spoilage. Identified during physical inventory count. Journal entry: Debit Inventory Shrinkage Expense / Credit Inventory. Under perpetual inventory, shrinkage is recognized immediately when discovered. Under periodic inventory, it's captured in the year-end count (lower ending inventory → higher COGS automatically). Shrinkage percentage = (Book Inventory − Physical Count) / Book Inventory × 100. Retail industry benchmark: ~1.5% of sales. Important internal control issue.
questions.highlight: Inventory shrinkage: Debit Loss / Credit Inventory. Perpetual detects it continuously; periodic annually.

questions.q: What is a chart of accounts entry for payroll liabilities?
questions.a:
Typical payroll liability GL accounts:
  • 2010 Federal Income Tax Withholding Payable
  • 2011 State Income Tax Withholding Payable
  • 2020 FICA — Social Security Payable (employee + employer)
  • 2021 FICA — Medicare Payable (employee + employer)
  • 2030 FUTA Payable
  • 2031 SUTA Payable
  • 2040 Health Insurance Premium Payable
  • 2050 401(k) Contributions Payable
  • 2060 Garnishments Payable
  • 2070 Accrued Wages Payable
questions.highlight: Payroll liabilities remain on balance sheet until remitted to IRS/state/vendor.

questions.q: What is QuickBooks and how is it used for bookkeeping?
questions.a: QuickBooks (Intuit) is the most widely used small business accounting software in the USA. Products: QuickBooks Online (QBO), QuickBooks Desktop, QuickBooks Enterprise. Key functions: invoicing, bill payment, bank reconciliation, payroll (add-on), financial reporting, tax prep integration. KPO firms commonly use QBO for US clients due to cloud access. Learning QuickBooks is essential for KPO accountants. Certifications: QuickBooks ProAdvisor. Common tasks: categorizing transactions, reconciling accounts, running P&L and balance sheet reports, managing payroll.
questions.highlight: QuickBooks ProAdvisor certification is highly valued for KPO bookkeeping professionals.

questions.q: What is a bank reconciliation for payroll accounts?
questions.a: Payroll bank reconciliation specifically reconciles the payroll clearing/bank account. Steps: (1) Match payroll checks/direct deposits issued per payroll register to checks cleared in bank. (2) Identify outstanding checks (issued but not cleared). (3) Confirm total cleared checks + outstanding checks = total payroll disbursements. (4) Verify tax deposit amounts cleared correctly. Payroll clearing accounts should typically have a zero balance after all checks clear. Uncleared stale checks need follow-up.
questions.highlight: Payroll bank account should reach near-zero balance after all checks clear each pay period.

questions.q: What is materiality in accounting?
questions.a: Materiality is an accounting concept where an item is material if its omission or misstatement could influence the economic decisions of users of the financial statements. There's no precise rule — judgment-based. Common benchmarks: 5–10% of pre-tax income, 0.5–2% of total assets or revenue. Immaterial items may be recorded using shortcuts (expense instead of capitalize, single entry instead of full allocation). Auditors use materiality to determine audit scope. KPO bookkeepers must understand that not every penny needs perfect accounting — practical judgment is required.
questions.highlight: Materiality: No fixed rule. Common benchmarks: 5% of pre-tax income or 1% of assets.

questions.q: What is the purpose of internal controls in bookkeeping?
questions.a:
Internal controls are policies and procedures to safeguard assets, ensure financial accuracy, prevent fraud, and ensure operational efficiency. Key controls:
  • Segregation of duties: person who approves payroll ≠ person who processes ≠ person who signs checks
  • Authorization: all payroll changes require approval
  • Physical controls: secure payroll files, restricted system access
  • Reconciliation: regular bank and GL reconciliation
  • Independent review: supervisory review of payroll register before payment
questions.highlight: Segregation of duties is the #1 payroll internal control. One person should not handle all payroll steps.

questions.q: What is a payroll clearing account?
questions.a: A payroll clearing account is a temporary bank account or GL account used as an intermediary between the operating bank account and actual payroll disbursements. Process: Transfer gross payroll amount to clearing account → pay employees (direct deposit from clearing) → pay taxes (from clearing to EFTPS) → clearing account reaches zero. Benefits: simplifies reconciliation, clearly isolates payroll costs, prevents commingling, easier to verify total payroll disbursement. Many companies use a dedicated payroll bank account as the clearing account.
questions.highlight: Payroll clearing account: Receives funded gross payroll, pays out employees and taxes, then zeros out.

questions.q: What is depreciation expense vs. accumulated depreciation?
questions.a: Depreciation Expense is the income statement account recording the period's depreciation — appears on P&L as an expense, reducing net income. Accumulated Depreciation is the balance sheet contra-asset account that totals all depreciation taken since asset acquisition. Book value = Cost − Accumulated Depreciation. Journal entry: Debit Depreciation Expense (P&L) / Credit Accumulated Depreciation (Balance Sheet). The expense is temporary (closed annually); accumulated depreciation grows each year until asset is retired.
questions.highlight: Depreciation Expense: Income Statement. Accumulated Depreciation: Balance Sheet (contra-asset).

questions.q: What is net present value (NPV) and how is it used?
questions.a: NPV calculates the present value of future cash flows minus the initial investment, using a discount rate (hurdle rate). Formula: NPV = Σ(Cash Flows / (1 + r)^t) − Initial Investment. Positive NPV = value-creating investment; negative NPV = destroys value. Used for capital budgeting decisions: should we buy equipment, expand, acquire? KPO accountants may prepare NPV analyses for clients' investment decisions. Related concept: IRR (Internal Rate of Return) — the discount rate that makes NPV = zero.
questions.highlight: NPV > 0: Accept the investment. NPV < 0: Reject. Discount rate = opportunity cost of capital.

questions.q: What are the accounting implications of payroll taxes on financial statements?
questions.a:
Payroll taxes affect financial statements in multiple ways:
  • Income Statement: Wages Expense + Payroll Tax Expense (FICA employer, FUTA, SUTA) = total labor cost
  • Balance Sheet: Accrued wages payable, FIT payable, FICA payable, FUTA/SUTA payable are current liabilities
  • Cash Flow Statement: Payroll cash disbursements appear in operating activities (both net pay and tax deposits)
Total payroll cost = Gross wages + Employer FICA + FUTA + SUTA + Benefits = Total labor burden.
questions.highlight: Total labor cost includes gross wages + 7.65% FICA + FUTA + SUTA + workers' comp + benefits.

questions.q: What is a suspense account in bookkeeping?
questions.a: A suspense account is a temporary GL account used when a transaction cannot be immediately classified to the correct account. Examples: receipt of payment where customer is unknown, invoice with unclear expense category, differences found during reconciliation. The amount 'parks' in suspense while investigation occurs. Must be cleared before financial statement preparation — unresolved suspense items indicate bookkeeping errors. Using suspense excessively signals poor processes. Payroll use: sometimes used for missing employee data.
questions.highlight: Suspense account: Temporary parking for unidentified transactions. Must be cleared at period-end.

questions.q: What is a management account and how does it differ from statutory accounts?
questions.a: Management accounts are internal financial reports prepared for business management decision-making — typically monthly, with detailed cost analysis, KPIs, departmental breakdowns, and variance analysis. No specific format required; designed for internal use. Statutory (financial) accounts are prepared under GAAP/IFRS for external stakeholders (shareholders, IRS, SEC) following required formats and disclosure rules. Most KPO firms prepare both types for clients — management accounts monthly, statutory accounts annually.
questions.highlight: Management accounts: Internal, flexible format, monthly. Statutory: External, GAAP/IFRS, annual.

questions.q: What is a trial balance and how does it differ from a balance sheet?
questions.a: A trial balance is an internal working document listing all GL accounts with their debit or credit balances — used to verify mathematical accuracy of the ledger. It includes temporary accounts (revenue, expenses) and permanent accounts. A balance sheet is a formal financial statement reporting only asset, liability, and equity accounts at a point in time — permanent accounts only, after closing entries. The balance sheet is derived from the adjusted trial balance after income statement accounts are closed to retained earnings.
questions.highlight: Trial balance includes ALL accounts. Balance sheet = only permanent accounts AFTER closing.

questions.q: What is a vendor reconciliation?
questions.a: Vendor reconciliation compares the company's AP subsidiary ledger balance for a vendor to the vendor's statement of account. Steps: (1) Request vendor statement; (2) Match each line item; (3) Identify: invoices on vendor statement not in company's system (need to be entered), invoices in company's system not on vendor statement (possibly paid or timing), payments not yet credited by vendor. Goal: resolve all differences. Prevents overpayment, duplicate payment, and missed invoices. KPO AP teams perform this monthly for major vendors.
questions.highlight: Vendor reconciliation: Match company's AP records to vendor's statement. Resolve all differences.

questions.q: What is the difference between bookkeeping and accounting?
questions.a: Bookkeeping is the recording of daily financial transactions (posting journal entries, reconciling bank accounts, maintaining ledgers, processing payroll). It's transactional and execution-focused. Accounting encompasses bookkeeping plus higher-level functions: interpreting, classifying, analyzing, summarizing, and reporting financial data; tax compliance; financial planning; auditing. KPO firms offer both: bookkeeping services (transaction processing) and accounting services (analysis, reporting, tax). Most KPO roles begin with bookkeeping and advance to accounting.
questions.highlight: Bookkeeping = recording transactions. Accounting = analysis, interpretation, and reporting.

questions.q: What are GAAP and why are they important?
questions.a: GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) are the standardized set of rules, standards, and procedures companies must follow when compiling financial statements in the USA. Issued by FASB (Financial Accounting Standards Board) through Accounting Standards Codification (ASC). GAAP ensures: comparability between companies, consistency over time, reliability of information, transparency for investors/creditors. Public companies must follow GAAP (SEC requirement). Private companies have more flexibility but GAAP is best practice. IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards) is used in 140+ countries.
questions.highlight: GAAP issued by FASB. Public companies required to follow. IFRS used internationally.

questions.q: What are the fundamental accounting principles under GAAP?
questions.a:
Key GAAP principles:
  • Revenue Recognition: Recognize when earned
  • Matching: Expenses in same period as related revenue
  • Historical Cost: Record assets at acquisition cost
  • Full Disclosure: Disclose all material information
  • Consistency: Same methods period to period
  • Conservatism: Anticipate losses; don't anticipate gains
  • Materiality: Only material items require strict compliance
  • Economic Entity: Keep business separate from owner's personal finances
  • Going Concern: Assume business will continue
questions.highlight: 10 GAAP principles. Matching + Revenue Recognition are most tested in interviews.

questions.q: What is ASC 606 (Revenue from Contracts with Customers)?
questions.a: ASC 606 (effective 2018–2019) replaced industry-specific revenue guidance with a single unified 5-step model: (1) Identify the contract with a customer; (2) Identify the distinct performance obligations; (3) Determine the transaction price; (4) Allocate transaction price to performance obligations; (5) Recognize revenue when/as each performance obligation is satisfied. Significantly changed recognition for software, construction, licensing, and multi-element arrangements. For payroll KPOs: recognize revenue per service period or per pay run processed.
questions.highlight: ASC 606 replaced all prior revenue guidance. 5-step model applies to ALL customer contracts.

questions.q: What is ASC 842 (Leases)?
questions.a: ASC 842 (Leases, effective 2019–2022) requires lessees to recognize most leases on the balance sheet as a Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset and corresponding Lease Liability. Previously, operating leases were off-balance sheet. Two types: Operating lease (straight-line expense, no amortization on face of P&L) and Finance lease (front-loaded expense — separate amortization + interest). Payroll KPO relevance: office leases now on balance sheet. Monthly journal: Debit Lease Liability + Interest Expense / Credit Cash; adjust ROU Asset amortization.
questions.highlight: ASC 842: Operating leases now ON balance sheet as ROU Asset + Lease Liability.

questions.q: What is ASC 718 (Stock-Based Compensation)?
questions.a: ASC 718 requires companies to recognize compensation expense for stock-based awards (stock options, RSUs, ESPPs) at fair value over the vesting period. Fair value at grant date determined using Black-Scholes or binomial models for options; grant-date FMV for RSUs. Journal entry each period: Debit Compensation Expense / Credit Additional Paid-In Capital. Payroll implication: when options vest/exercise, coordinate with stock admin team to report W-2 income properly. KPO firms assist with monthly stock compensation expense calculations.
questions.highlight: ASC 718: Stock comp expensed at fair value over vesting. Coordinate equity plan with payroll W-2.

questions.q: What is ASC 805 (Business Combinations)?
questions.a: ASC 805 governs accounting for acquisitions. The acquirer must use the acquisition method: recognize identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed at fair value on acquisition date; any excess purchase price = goodwill. Payroll implications: acquired employees' benefit obligations (pension, OPEB) must be valued; accrued wages and payroll taxes of target assumed at fair value; change-in-control payments (Section 280G golden parachutes) may be triggered. KPO firms help clients with Day 1 payroll integration.
questions.highlight: ASC 805 acquisition method: All assets/liabilities at fair value. Payroll obligations assumed at FV.

questions.q: What is the concept of conservatism in accounting?
questions.a: Conservatism (also called prudence) is a GAAP principle requiring accountants to choose solutions that result in lower asset values and revenue, and higher liabilities and expenses, when alternatives exist. Anticipate losses; don't anticipate gains. Examples: lower of cost or net realizable value for inventory, allowance for doubtful accounts estimated conservatively, contingent liabilities recorded when probable. This prevents overstating company performance. However, extreme conservatism that creates hidden reserves is not acceptable under GAAP.
questions.highlight: Conservatism: When in doubt, record the worse outcome. Never overstate assets or income.

questions.q: What is going concern and how does it affect accounting?
questions.a: Going concern is the assumption that a business will continue to operate for the foreseeable future (at least 12 months from the balance sheet date) and will not liquidate. Under this assumption, assets are reported at cost/book value, not liquidation value. If substantial doubt exists about going concern, management must disclose it in financial statements. Auditors issue going concern opinions. Payroll implications: if going concern is in doubt, employees need advance notice under WARN Act (60 days for 50+ employee layoffs).
questions.highlight: Going concern assumption: Business will continue 12+ months. Disclosure required when doubt exists.

questions.q: What is the full disclosure principle?
questions.a: The full disclosure principle requires that financial statements disclose all information that would be material to the decisions of a knowledgeable financial statement user. Disclosures appear in: notes to financial statements, management discussion and analysis (MD&A), supplementary schedules. Examples: accounting policies, contingent liabilities, related party transactions, subsequent events, stock-based compensation details. For KPO: payroll-related disclosures include stock compensation expense, pension obligations, and significant changes in employee benefit plans.
questions.highlight: Full disclosure: If it's material and would change a user's decision, it must be disclosed.

questions.q: What is the historical cost principle?
questions.a: The historical cost principle requires that assets be recorded at their original acquisition cost, not at current market value. Exceptions: some financial instruments (mark-to-market), biological assets (IFRS), and impaired assets (written down but not up). The principle provides objectivity and verifiability — the purchase price is documented. For KPO bookkeepers: equipment purchased in Year 1 stays at cost minus accumulated depreciation regardless of market value changes. Only impairment (value drops below book) triggers adjustment downward.
questions.highlight: Historical cost: Record at what you paid, not what it's worth today. Verifiable and objective.

questions.q: What is goodwill impairment testing?
questions.a: Goodwill (excess purchase price over net assets in acquisitions) must be tested for impairment at least annually (and when triggering events occur). Under ASC 350, Step 1: Compare the reporting unit's fair value to its carrying amount. If fair value < carrying amount, record impairment = difference (up to goodwill balance). No amortization for public companies under GAAP (private companies can elect amortization under ASC 350-20). Impairment is a non-cash charge on income statement. Payroll KPO role: ensure payroll allocations are correct per reporting unit for testing purposes.
questions.highlight: Goodwill: Not amortized under GAAP (except private company election). Tested annually for impairment.

questions.q: What is IFRS and how does it differ from US GAAP?
questions.a: IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards) is issued by IASB, used in 140+ countries. Key differences from US GAAP: IFRS is principles-based; GAAP is rules-based. IFRS allows LIFO inventory (GAAP prohibits LIFO — wait, IFRS PROHIBITS LIFO; GAAP allows it). IFRS permits upward revaluation of PP&E; GAAP doesn't. IFRS has fewer bright-line rules for leases and revenue. US public companies use GAAP; foreign private issuers filing with SEC can use IFRS. KPO firms serving international clients must know both.
questions.highlight: IFRS prohibits LIFO inventory. GAAP allows LIFO. IFRS more principles-based; GAAP more rules-based.

questions.q: What is the economic entity concept?
questions.a: The economic entity concept (also called entity concept or separate entity assumption) requires that business transactions be kept completely separate from the personal transactions of the business owners. The business is treated as a distinct economic unit. This is fundamental for all accounting: recording personal expenses as business expenses violates this principle and is tax fraud. In payroll: owner's personal draws/distributions are not payroll (no FICA in S-corps/partnerships for pass-through income).
questions.highlight: Entity concept: Business finances strictly separate from owner's personal finances.

questions.q: What is the monetary unit assumption?
questions.a: The monetary unit assumption states that only transactions expressible in money (currency) are recorded in accounting, and that the monetary unit remains stable over time (ignoring inflation). This is why accounting records don't capture employee morale, brand reputation, or intellectual capital unless specifically purchased. Also why inflation adjustments are generally not made to historical cost figures under US GAAP. IFRS has some inflation adjustments for hyperinflationary economies.
questions.highlight: Monetary unit assumption: Only money-measurable items in books. Ignores inflation under GAAP.

questions.q: What is the time period assumption?
questions.a: The time period assumption divides an entity's continuous life into artificial time periods for reporting purposes — monthly, quarterly, and annual periods. This enables interim reporting, tax filings, and performance evaluation. It's what necessitates accruals, deferrals, and adjusting entries — because transactions often span multiple periods. For KPO firms: monthly management accounts, quarterly tax filings, and annual financial statements all result from this assumption.
questions.highlight: Time period assumption: Artificial periods (month/quarter/year) enable periodic financial reporting.

questions.q: What are contingent liabilities and how are they recorded?
questions.a: A contingent liability is a potential obligation dependent on a future event. Under ASC 450: (1) Probable and estimable → Accrue: Debit Loss / Credit Liability. (2) Probable but not estimable → Disclose in notes only. (3) Reasonably possible → Disclose in notes. (4) Remote → No disclosure required. Common examples: pending lawsuits, product warranties, IRS audits. Payroll-related contingencies: employment discrimination lawsuits, worker misclassification claims, unpaid overtime class actions.
questions.highlight: Contingent liability: Probable + estimable = accrue. Reasonably possible = disclose. Remote = nothing.

questions.q: What is the difference between tangible and intangible assets?
questions.a: Tangible assets have physical substance: machinery, buildings, vehicles, computers, furniture. Recorded at cost, depreciated over useful life. Intangible assets lack physical form: patents, trademarks, copyrights, goodwill, customer relationships, software. Recorded at cost; amortized (if finite life) or tested for impairment annually (if indefinite life like goodwill). For payroll/KPO: internally developed software costs have specific capitalization rules (ASC 350-40 for cloud computing arrangements).
questions.highlight: Intangibles: Finite life → amortize. Indefinite life (goodwill, trademarks) → annual impairment test.

questions.q: What is MACRS depreciation for tax purposes?
questions.a: MACRS (Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System) is the IRS-mandated depreciation system for tax purposes, different from GAAP depreciation. Assigns property to recovery periods: 5-year (computers, cars), 7-year (office furniture, most equipment), 15-year (land improvements), 27.5-year (residential rental), 39-year (commercial). Uses declining balance switching to straight-line. Also: Section 179 expensing (up to $1.16M in 2024) and bonus depreciation (60% in 2024, phasing down). MACRS creates book-tax differences → deferred tax liabilities.
questions.highlight: MACRS creates temporary differences: faster depreciation for tax = DTL on books.

questions.q: What is the effective interest method for amortizing bond discount/premium?
questions.a: The effective interest method allocates interest expense proportional to the carrying amount of the debt at the stated effective rate. For bonds issued at discount: interest expense > coupon paid; difference increases carrying value. For premium: interest expense < coupon; difference decreases carrying value. Used by both GAAP and IFRS (preferred over straight-line). Journal for discount bond each period: Debit Interest Expense (at effective rate × carrying value) / Credit Cash (coupon) / Credit Bond Discount (plug). Book value approaches face value over time.
questions.highlight: Effective interest: Interest expense = effective rate × carrying value. Preferred over straight-line.

questions.q: What is LIFO vs FIFO inventory accounting?
questions.a: FIFO (First In, First Out): Oldest costs assigned to COGS; newest costs stay in ending inventory. In rising prices: lower COGS, higher profits, higher taxes. LIFO (Last In, First Out): Newest costs assigned to COGS; oldest costs stay in inventory. In rising prices: higher COGS, lower profits, lower taxes. LIFO is only allowed under US GAAP (prohibited under IFRS). LIFO reserve = difference between FIFO and LIFO inventory values. Many US companies used LIFO to reduce taxes before LIFO conformity rules.
questions.highlight: LIFO: Tax advantage in rising prices. Only allowed under US GAAP — prohibited by IFRS.

questions.q: What is a variable interest entity (VIE) under ASC 810?
questions.a: A VIE is an entity in which the investor holds a controlling interest not based on the majority of voting rights (common in SPEs, structured finance). Under ASC 810, a company must consolidate a VIE if it is the primary beneficiary — the party that: (1) has power to direct activities that most significantly impact the VIE's economic performance, and (2) receives most of the benefits/absorbs most of the losses. Common in leasing entities, franchises, and healthcare. KPO accountants must understand VIEs to properly consolidate financial statements.
questions.highlight: VIE consolidation: Power over activities + exposure to significant economics = must consolidate.

questions.q: What is segment reporting under ASC 280?
questions.a: ASC 280 requires public companies to report financial information about operating segments — components of a business whose operating results are regularly reviewed by the chief operating decision maker (CODM). Reportable segment: 10%+ of revenue, assets, or profit/loss. Disclosures: revenue by segment, segment profit/loss, significant assets. For KPO purposes: multi-entity clients may have multiple segments requiring separate reporting. Payroll allocation across segments is critical for accurate segment profitability.
questions.highlight: Reportable segment: 10%+ of revenue OR assets OR profit/loss. CODM determines segment structure.

questions.q: What is the difference between operating and non-operating income?
questions.a: Operating income (EBIT) comes from core business activities: revenue minus COGS minus operating expenses (SG&A, R&D, depreciation). Non-operating income/expense comes from peripheral activities: interest income/expense, gains/losses on asset sales, foreign exchange gains/losses, investment income. KPO significance: payroll costs are operating expenses (SG&A or COGS depending on employee role). Interest on payroll tax liabilities (late deposits) would be non-operating expense. Proper classification is essential for ratio analysis.
questions.highlight: Operating income = core business. Non-operating = interest, gains/losses, FX. Classify payroll correctly.

questions.q: What is retained earnings and how is it calculated?
questions.a: Retained earnings is the cumulative net income kept in the business (not distributed as dividends) since inception. Formula: Ending Retained Earnings = Beginning Retained Earnings + Net Income − Dividends. Appears in the equity section of the balance sheet. Negative retained earnings (accumulated deficit) indicates cumulative losses exceed cumulative profits — common in startups. For KPO: ensure payroll expenses are properly recorded so net income and retained earnings are accurate.
questions.highlight: Retained Earnings = Beginning RE + Net Income − Dividends. Negative = accumulated deficit.

questions.q: What is the difference between gross and net method for recording purchase discounts?
questions.a: When vendors offer early payment discounts (2/10 net 30 = 2% off if paid in 10 days): Gross method: Record full invoice amount; if discount taken, credit Purchase Discounts. Net method: Record net amount immediately; if discount not taken, debit Purchase Discounts Lost (expense). Net method better reflects economic reality — discounts not taken are a financing cost. GAAP allows both; net method is preferred by accountants because discounts lost are explicitly tracked.
questions.highlight: Net method is preferred: Lost discounts = interest expense. Gross method: Discounts taken = income.

questions.q: What is earnings per share (EPS) and how is it calculated?
questions.a: EPS measures profit attributable to each common share. Basic EPS = Net Income Available to Common Shareholders / Weighted Average Common Shares. Diluted EPS accounts for dilutive securities (options, warrants, convertible notes) that could increase share count. Diluted EPS ≤ Basic EPS. Required disclosure for public companies. Payroll connection: stock-based compensation (RSUs, options) increases dilutive shares outstanding, reducing diluted EPS. Non-employee stock options also included.
questions.highlight: Diluted EPS < Basic EPS when dilutive securities exist. Stock options reduce diluted EPS.

questions.q: What is the concept of comparability and consistency in GAAP?
questions.a: Consistency requires using the same accounting methods from period to period to allow comparability over time within a company. Changes in accounting methods (e.g., switching from FIFO to LIFO) require disclosure and retroactive restatement in most cases. Comparability allows users to compare financial statements across different companies by ensuring similar events are reported similarly. These principles are why auditors scrutinize accounting method changes.
questions.highlight: Consistency: Same method year-over-year. Changes require disclosure + often retroactive adjustment.

questions.q: What is pension accounting under ASC 715?
questions.a: ASC 715 governs defined benefit pension accounting. Companies must recognize: pension obligation (PBO — Projected Benefit Obligation), plan assets, and net pension liability/asset on balance sheet. Annual pension cost includes: service cost, interest cost, expected return on plan assets, and amortization of actuarial gains/losses. The funded status = Fair Value of Plan Assets − PBO. Under- or overfunded plans appear on balance sheet. Actuarial assumptions (discount rate, salary growth, mortality) significantly impact pension liability. KPO firms help calculate pension expense.
questions.highlight: Funded status = Plan Assets FV − PBO. Underfunded = liability. Service cost = current year benefit earned.

questions.q: What is the concept of substance over form in accounting?
questions.a: Substance over form requires that transactions be accounted for according to their economic substance rather than their legal form. Example: a sale-leaseback transaction that economically retains all risks/rewards of ownership is accounted for as a financing arrangement (not a sale). Another example: a lease structured to avoid capitalization but effectively a purchase must be capitalized. ASC 842 embodies this. KPO application: related party transactions between owner and company should be recorded based on economic substance, not just legal structure.
questions.highlight: Substance over form: Record economic reality, not just legal structure.

questions.q: What is the lower of cost or net realizable value (LCNRV) rule?
questions.a: LCNRV requires inventory to be reported at the lower of its cost or net realizable value. Net realizable value (NRV) = estimated selling price minus reasonably predictable costs of completion, disposal, and transportation. Under ASC 330, GAAP requires LCNRV for all inventory (LIFO/retail method inventory still uses lower of cost or market — different calculation). If inventory's NRV falls below cost, write it down: Debit Inventory Loss / Credit Inventory. Conservatism principle prevents overstating assets.
questions.highlight: LCNRV: If NRV < Cost, write down inventory. Debit Loss / Credit Inventory. Conservatism.

questions.q: What is the comprehensive income concept?
questions.a: Comprehensive income is net income plus other comprehensive income (OCI). OCI includes changes in equity not from transactions with owners and not included in net income: unrealized gains/losses on available-for-sale securities, pension plan adjustments, foreign currency translation adjustments, effective portion of cash flow hedge gains/losses. Reported in the Statement of Comprehensive Income (can be combined with income statement or separate). Accumulated OCI (AOCI) is a component of stockholders' equity on the balance sheet.
questions.highlight: Comprehensive income = Net Income + OCI. OCI includes pension adjustments, FX translation, unrealized gains.

questions.q: What is the difference between current and non-current assets/liabilities?
questions.a: Current: Expected to be converted to cash, used up, or due within one year or one operating cycle (whichever is longer). Examples: cash, AR, inventory, prepaid expenses, current portion of long-term debt, accrued liabilities. Non-current: Beyond one year. Examples: PP&E, goodwill, long-term investments, deferred tax assets/liabilities, long-term debt. Balance sheet classification affects: working capital analysis, liquidity ratios, and loan covenant compliance.
questions.highlight: Current = within 1 year. Non-current = beyond 1 year. Affects liquidity ratios and covenants.

questions.q: What is a common size financial statement?
questions.a: A common size financial statement expresses all line items as a percentage of a base amount, enabling meaningful comparison across different-sized companies or across periods. For income statement: each line item as % of total revenue. For balance sheet: each line item as % of total assets. Example: COGS/Revenue, SG&A/Revenue, Payroll expense/Revenue. KPO firms use common size analysis to benchmark client financial ratios against industry norms and identify cost structure issues.
questions.highlight: Common size: Express all P&L items as % of revenue; balance sheet items as % of total assets.

questions.q: What is the difference between book value and fair value?
questions.a: Book value (carrying value): Value of an asset as reported on the balance sheet — original cost minus accumulated depreciation/amortization. May differ significantly from market value over time. Fair value: The price a willing buyer would pay a willing seller in an arm's-length transaction (market-based measurement). GAAP generally uses historical cost but requires fair value for: business combinations, impairment testing, certain financial instruments, and pension assets.
questions.highlight: Book value = Cost − Accumulated Depreciation. Fair value = what market would pay today.

questions.q: What are related party transactions and how must they be disclosed?
questions.a: Related party transactions are dealings between entities with a pre-existing relationship: parent-subsidiary, common ownership, key management and company, board members. Under ASC 850, must disclose: nature of relationship, description of transaction, dollar amounts, amounts due to/from related parties. Payroll relevance: officer compensation, owner loans, rents paid to owner-controlled entities, family member employment. These are frequently scrutinized in audits and tax examinations. Cannot assume arm's-length pricing — must document and disclose.
questions.highlight: ASC 850: Disclose all related party transactions — nature, amount, and outstanding balances.

questions.q: What is the difference between EBIT and EBITDA?
questions.a: EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes) = Net Income + Interest Expense + Income Tax Expense. Represents operating profitability. Also = Operating Income. EBITDA adds back Depreciation and Amortization to EBIT. EBITDA is a proxy for operating cash flow — it removes non-cash expenses (D&A) and financing decisions (interest) and tax environment from comparisons. EBITDA is not a GAAP measure. Limitation: EBITDA ignores capital expenditure requirements, which can be substantial.
questions.highlight: EBIT = Operating income. EBITDA = EBIT + D&A. Neither is GAAP but both used widely in finance.

questions.q: What is the cost of capital in accounting/finance?
questions.a: Cost of capital is the minimum return a company must earn on its investments to satisfy its investors. Composed of: cost of debt (after-tax interest rate) and cost of equity (risk-based return required by shareholders). Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) = (Equity Weight × Cost of Equity) + (Debt Weight × After-tax Cost of Debt). Used as the discount rate in NPV analysis and company valuation. For KPO accountants: understanding WACC helps evaluate capital budgeting decisions and M&A valuations.
questions.highlight: WACC = blended cost of debt + equity financing. Used as NPV discount rate and valuation benchmark.

questions.q: What is a quality of earnings analysis?
questions.a: Quality of earnings analysis assesses whether reported earnings truly reflect the company's underlying economic performance. KPO and accounting professionals examine: revenue recognition timing (is revenue being pulled forward?), accrual reversals, changes in accounting estimates, one-time items inflating operating income, working capital changes, capex vs. maintenance distinction, related party transactions. High quality earnings = sustainable, repeatable, cash-backed income. Low quality = aggressive estimates, one-time items, non-cash-backed revenue.
questions.highlight: Quality of earnings: Is revenue real, recurring, and cash-backed? Or accounting-driven?

questions.q: What is the difference between a direct and indirect cash flow method?
questions.a: Indirect method (most common): Starts with net income, adds back non-cash items (depreciation, amortization, stock comp), adjusts for working capital changes (ΔAR, ΔAP, ΔInventory). Simple and tied to income statement. Direct method (preferred by IFRS): Shows actual cash collections and payments — cash received from customers, cash paid to suppliers, cash paid to employees. More informative but requires detailed tracking. GAAP allows either; FASB prefers direct but most companies use indirect because data is more readily available.
questions.highlight: Indirect method: Net income → adjust for non-cash + WC changes. Most common. FASB prefers direct.

questions.q: What is ASC 740 (Income Taxes)?
questions.a: ASC 740 governs accounting for income taxes. Key concepts: (1) Current tax expense: taxes payable on current year taxable income; (2) Deferred tax assets and liabilities: arising from temporary differences between GAAP book income and taxable income; (3) Valuation allowance: reduces DTA when it's more likely than not that some/all won't be realized; (4) FIN 48/ASC 740-10: uncertain tax positions must be recorded if more-likely-than-not of being sustained. Payroll connection: stock option exercises, RSU vestings, and NQDC distributions create book-tax differences tracked under ASC 740.
questions.highlight: ASC 740: Current + deferred tax expense. Valuation allowance when DTA unlikely realizable.

questions.q: What are the GAAP requirements for disclosure of commitments?
questions.a: GAAP requires disclosure of significant commitments in the notes to financial statements — future obligations not yet required to be recorded as liabilities. Examples: future minimum lease payments (now partially on-balance sheet under ASC 842), long-term purchase commitments, employment contracts, capital expenditure commitments, royalty arrangements. These commitments affect cash flow forecasting and user's understanding of future obligations. For KPO: multi-year employment agreements with executives are material commitments requiring disclosure.
questions.highlight: Commitments: Future obligations not yet liabilities. Disclose in notes. Leases, purchases, employment.

questions.q: What is the difference between accrued expenses and accounts payable?
questions.a: Accounts Payable (AP): Specific, invoiced amounts owed to vendors with known payment terms. Example: $5,000 rent invoice received. Accrued Expenses: Estimated amounts incurred but not yet invoiced or payable. Example: $2,000 wages earned last 3 days of month, paid next period; $800 accrued interest on loan. Both are current liabilities but AP has a specific invoice while accrued expenses are estimated. Year-end payroll accruals (wages earned Dec 28–31) are the most common accrued expense on the balance sheet.
questions.highlight: AP = invoiced, known amount. Accrued expenses = earned but not yet invoiced. Both are current liabilities.

questions.q: What is XBRL and its relevance to accounting/reporting?
questions.a: XBRL (eXtensible Business Reporting Language) is an XML-based digital language for financial reporting. The SEC requires US public companies to tag financial statement data with XBRL taxonomy codes, enabling machine-readable financial reporting. Each line item in the financial statements gets a specific XBRL tag. Enables automated analysis, comparison, and data extraction. KPO firms servicing public companies must understand XBRL tagging or use software that generates it automatically. iXBRL (inline XBRL) is now required for SEC filings.
questions.highlight: XBRL: SEC-required machine-readable tagging of financial statements. Required for public companies.

questions.q: What is the difference between GAAP restatement and revision?
questions.a: Restatement: Correction of a material error in previously issued financial statements — required when prior period statements are misstated. Must be retroactively corrected in comparative statements with disclosure. Serious — suggests control failure. Revision: Immaterial error corrected in current period, or voluntary change in accounting estimate. Prospective treatment for estimate changes; retrospective for most policy changes. SEC enforcement action risk for fraudulent restatements. KPO teams must have robust review processes to prevent errors requiring restatement.
questions.highlight: Restatement = material error correction, retroactive. Estimate change = prospective adjustment.

questions.q: What is activity-based costing (ABC)?
questions.a: Activity-Based Costing (ABC) assigns overhead costs to products/services based on activities that drive those costs, rather than a single overhead rate based on direct labor hours. ABC steps: (1) Identify activities; (2) Assign cost to each activity pool; (3) Determine cost driver for each activity; (4) Assign costs to products based on activity consumption. More accurate for complex operations with diverse products. KPO application: ABC can determine actual cost per payroll process, per client, or per transaction type — enabling better client pricing decisions.
questions.highlight: ABC costing: Assigns overhead based on actual activities, not just labor hours. More accurate.

questions.q: What is a budget variance and how is it analyzed?
questions.a:
A budget variance is the difference between budgeted (planned) amounts and actual results. Types:
  • Favorable variance: Actual cost < Budget (expenses) or Actual revenue > Budget
  • Unfavorable/Adverse variance: Opposite of above
